Flows are characterized by materials moving like a viscous fluid, including debris flows, earthflows, and mudflows. Complex landslides combine two or more types of movement. Classification is important for hazard mapping and designing mitigation measures.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: The causes of landslides are both natural and human-induced. Natural causes include geological weaknesses such as weathered rock, steep slopes, and unstable soils. Triggering factors include intense or prolonged rainfall that saturates soil, earthquakes that shake and destabilize slopes, volcanic eruptions producing debris avalanches, and freeze-thaw cycles that weaken rock. Anthropogenic causes include deforestation, slope cutting for roads and settlements, unscientific mining and quarrying, improper drainage, and loading slopes with heavy structures. Often, multiple factors interact to initiate failure, such as rainfall on deforested slopes or earthquakes on poorly drained terrain.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Landslides are governed by processes of shear stress, slope angle, and resisting strength of soil and rock. When driving forces such as gravity, pore water pressure, or seismic loading exceed resisting forces such as cohesion, friction, and root reinforcement, failure occurs. Movement can be shallow or deep, slow or rapid. The mechanisms include block sliding, rotational slumping, debris avalanching, and fluidization of materials. Understanding mechanisms is crucial for designing engineering interventions such as retaining walls, slope reinforcement, and drainage systems.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: India experiences diverse types of landslides due to varied geology and climate. Rockfalls and rock avalanches are common in the Himalayas. Debris flows occur in the northeast and Western Ghats during heavy monsoon rains. Earthflows and mudslides are frequent in the Nilgiris. Submarine landslides have been noted in offshore regions. Anthropogenic landslides occur near road cuts, construction zones, and mining areas. The classification of landslides in India helps prioritize mitigation in different terrain types, from high mountain slopes to coastal bluffs.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Nearly 15 percent of India’s land area, about 0.49 million square kilometers, is prone to landslides. The most vulnerable regions are the Himalayan states, the northeast, the Nilgiris, and the Western and Eastern Ghats. States like Jammu and Kashmir, Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Arunachal Pradesh, Sikkim, and Kerala are highly exposed. Frequent landslides occur along major highways, pilgrim routes, and near hydropower projects. In urban areas like Mumbai, slope failures in densely populated zones pose risks to human life. Mapping and zonation studies by the Geological Survey of India provide insights into these high-risk areas.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: India has witnessed many catastrophic landslides. The 1893 Gohna lake landslide in Uttarakhand blocked the Birahiganga river, creating a dam that later burst and caused devastating floods. The 1970 landslide on the Patalganga river triggered the Alaknanda tragedy. The Malpa landslide of 1998 killed over 200 people and destroyed the village. The 2003 Varunavat landslide threatened Uttarkashi town for weeks. The 2005 Konkan landslides in Maharashtra killed over 100 people. In 2010, debris flows in Leh destroyed homes and killed more than 100. Numerous recurring landslides in Darjeeling, Sikkim, and the northeast continue to cause economic losses and fatalities, highlighting the chronic nature of this hazard.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Globally, many landslides have caused catastrophic impacts. In 1970, the Yungay landslide in Peru, triggered by an earthquake, buried the city and killed 17,000 people. In Japan, debris flows kill about 90 people annually. The 2005 Kashmir earthquake triggered thousands of slope failures across India and Pakistan. Canada’s Cordillera has suffered multiple large rock avalanches and debris flows, with the 1903 Frank Slide as the worst in Canadian history. In recent decades, events in Indonesia, Costa Rica, and China have killed thousands and disrupted infrastructure. These global examples demonstrate the destructive power of landslides and the need for systematic management.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Landslides have immediate and long-term impacts. Short-term effects include death, injury, loss of homes, and damage to infrastructure, cultural sites, and heritage buildings. Long-term effects include permanent landscape alteration, loss of farmland, soil erosion, silting of reservoirs, and reduced hydroelectric capacity. Landslides frequently block highways and disrupt transport in the Himalayan and northeastern regions, isolating communities for days. Landslide dams cause upstream flooding and catastrophic downstream floods if breached. Economically, India loses about Rs. 300 crores annually, while globally, billions of dollars are lost each year.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Human activities significantly contribute to landslide risk. Deforestation removes stabilizing vegetation. Unscientific road cutting destabilizes slopes. Mining and quarrying in areas such as the Doon Valley, Almora, and Pithoragarh have stripped protective cover, causing slope failures. Unplanned urban growth in hilly regions increases exposure. Expansion of settlements, dams, tunnels, and reservoirs adds loads to unstable slopes. Poor drainage and dumping of debris on slopes or into rivers exacerbate hazards. Lack of hazard awareness and absence of detailed landslide maps leave communities unprepared when landslides occur in new areas.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Landslides often interact with other disasters, occurring as secondary effects or amplifying impacts. Earthquakes in the Himalaya and northeast India routinely trigger thousands of co-seismic landslides. Floods and cloudbursts in Uttarakhand, Himachal, and the northeast have caused debris flows and slope failures. Landslides contribute to dam and reservoir siltation, reducing storage capacity. They can also block rivers, creating temporary dams that later breach to produce catastrophic flooding. In urban centers, slope failures during monsoons create additional flooding by obstructing drainage. These interconnections make landslides an integral part of multi-hazard risk assessment.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Historically, landslide management in India has been site-specific and reactive. Immediate measures such as debris clearance were adopted, but long-term strategies were limited. Recognizing the national importance of the problem, the National Disaster Management Authority has initiated guidelines and policies for landslide risk reduction. The National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM) is leading training programs, hazard assessment, and awareness campaigns. The Geological Survey of India (GSI) conducts hazard zonation mapping. Despite these efforts, institutional capacity varies greatly across states, and coordination has been limited. A comprehensive national strategy is increasingly seen as necessary to integrate landslide risk management into development and climate adaptation frameworks.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: The introductory sub-module consolidates understanding of landslides, their classification, processes, triggers, and impacts in India and worldwide. India is highly vulnerable, with nearly 15 percent of its territory prone to landslides and hundreds of fatalities annually. Globally, catastrophic landslides have caused tens of thousands of deaths and multi-billion-dollar damages. Both natural and anthropogenic factors contribute to risk, and landslides interact with earthquakes, floods, and other hazards. Current management has been fragmented, but institutional frameworks such as NDMA and NIDM are building capacity. A multidisciplinary and multi-hazard approach is needed to reduce risk, combining scientific knowledge, engineering measures, ecological restoration, and community awareness.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: The geological causes of landslides arise from the inherent characteristics of rock, soil, and slope formations. Weak or weathered rock strata, heavily jointed or fractured formations, and soils with high clay or silt content are naturally prone to slope failures. Regions with steep slopes above 30 degrees are especially unstable, and the presence of ancient landslide scars often indicates susceptibility to renewed failure. Geological structures such as faults, shear zones, and bedding planes can create planes of weakness that facilitate sliding. In the Himalayas, young tectonic formations with fragile sedimentary and metamorphic rocks contribute to frequent slope failures. In the Nilgiris, deeply weathered soils and residual laterites collapse under saturation. Volcanic terrains, with unconsolidated ash deposits, are also prone to rapid debris avalanches. These inherent geological weaknesses provide the baseline conditions that are easily exploited by rainfall, earthquakes, and human disturbance, resulting in frequent landslides.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Rainfall and hydrology are the most significant immediate triggers of landslides. Intense or prolonged rainfall saturates soil, increases pore water pressure, reduces shear strength, and leads to slope collapse. Cloudbursts in the Himalayas and Western Ghats frequently trigger massive debris flows and flash floods. Monsoon rains account for most landslides in India, especially in Uttarakhand, Himachal, the northeast, Kerala, and the Nilgiris. Snowmelt and rapid thawing in spring also destabilize slopes in high-altitude regions. Groundwater seepage into fractures or between bedding planes lubricates weak zones and causes slow creep or sudden failure. Hydrological imbalances from blocked drainage, leaking pipes, or poorly managed irrigation further aggravate risks. The frequency and magnitude of rainfall-triggered landslides are expected to increase under climate change, with more extreme rainfall events projected in Himalayan and coastal states.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Earthquakes are major triggers of co-seismic landslides, especially in tectonically active regions like the Himalayas and northeast India. The Shillong earthquake of 1897, Kangra in 1905, Bihar-Nepal in 1934, Assam in 1950, Uttarkashi in 1991, Chamoli in 1999, Kashmir in 2005, and Sikkim in 2011 all caused widespread slope failures. These ranged from shallow rockfalls to massive landslide dams blocking rivers. Earthquake shaking reduces soil cohesion, induces liquefaction in water-saturated sands, and creates tension cracks that later collapse. Volcanic eruptions also cause landslides by destabilizing ash-laden slopes and generating lahars—volcanic debris flows mixed with water. The 1980 eruption of Mount St. Helens in the U.S. produced the largest historic landslide ever recorded, releasing 2.5 km³ of rock and debris. In India, while active volcanoes are absent, tectonically active zones mimic similar instability risks.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Human activities have dramatically increased landslide risk in India. Deforestation for agriculture, logging, and urban expansion removes stabilizing vegetation, exposing soils to erosion. Road cutting in Himalayan and Western Ghats terrain destabilizes slopes by creating artificial oversteepening without adequate retaining structures. Unscientific mining and quarrying in the Doon Valley, Jhiroli, and Chandhak have caused severe slope failures and loss of forest cover. Unplanned urbanization on steep slopes, like in Mumbai’s suburban hills, exposes large populations to monsoon-triggered landslides. Hydropower dams, tunnels, and reservoirs add loads and water pressures that destabilize fragile slopes, as seen in the Alaknanda and Nathpa-Jhakhri cases. Poor drainage management, debris dumping, and unsafe land-use practices further exacerbate landslide frequency. Human influence often converts otherwise stable slopes into highly hazardous zones.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: The mechanics of landslides involve the balance between driving forces such as gravity, pore water pressure, and seismic loading, versus resisting forces such as soil cohesion, internal friction, and vegetation reinforcement. When driving forces exceed resisting forces, slope failure occurs. Mechanisms vary by material and slope: rotational slumps occur on clay-rich soils; translational slides occur on bedding planes or rock joints; debris avalanches involve rapid downslope flow of unconsolidated material; mudflows behave like viscous fluids after saturation; creep is slow, imperceptible downslope movement that eventually destabilizes structures. Landslide progression often follows a cycle: initial cracking, slow creep, acceleration under rainfall or seismic stress, sudden failure, and eventual deposition. Understanding mechanisms enables engineers to design retaining walls, check dams, soil nailing, rock bolting, and drainage measures that improve stability.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Climate change and multi-hazard environments amplify landslide risks. Increasingly intense monsoons, cloudbursts, and unseasonal heavy rainfall events saturate slopes and trigger more frequent landslides. Glacial melt in the Himalayas destabilizes moraines, producing glacial lake outburst floods and slope failures. Combined disasters such as earthquakes followed by rainfall or floods exacerbate slope instability, as seen in Uttarakhand where tremors opened cracks that later collapsed in heavy rain. Wildfires followed by rainfall also trigger debris flows as vegetation loss exposes bare soil. These interactions highlight that landslide risk must be managed within a multi-hazard framework, integrating flood, earthquake, wildfire, and climate adaptation strategies.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Landslide Hazard Zonation (LHZ) refers to the process of dividing a geographic area into zones based on the degree of actual or potential hazard from landslides. It is a critical tool for assessing susceptibility and for guiding land-use planning, engineering projects, and disaster preparedness. The objective of LHZ mapping is to identify and categorize areas into high, moderate, and low hazard classes, helping policymakers, engineers, and communities to make informed decisions. In India, the Geological Survey of India (GSI) has been the nodal agency conducting LHZ mapping in different parts of the Himalayas, the northeast, and other landslide-prone regions. These maps combine geological, geomorphological, hydrological, and anthropogenic parameters to provide a composite hazard assessment.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Several approaches are used for landslide hazard zonation. Qualitative approaches rely on expert interpretation of geological and geomorphic conditions, slope categories, vegetation, and drainage. Quantitative approaches assign numerical values to different causative factors, generating hazard indices through weighted overlays or statistical models. Deterministic methods use geotechnical stability analysis to compute safety factors for slopes. Probabilistic methods estimate likelihood of landslides based on historical frequency and triggering events. Advanced GIS-based multi-criteria evaluation integrates diverse layers such as slope angle, soil type, rainfall intensity, land use, drainage density, and seismicity. Remote sensing data, including LiDAR, InSAR, and high-resolution satellite imagery, are increasingly used to improve accuracy. The chosen method depends on scale: large-scale maps for regional planning, medium-scale for district-level development, and detailed site-specific maps for engineering projects.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: LHZ mapping in India has been widely applied in development planning, infrastructure design, and disaster risk reduction. The Geological Survey of India has mapped large sections of the Himalayas, the Western Ghats, and the northeast. These maps guide the alignment of highways, railways, hydropower dams, and tunnels in landslide-prone terrain. They help in identifying safe zones for settlements and in prioritizing slope stabilization measures. State governments use LHZ maps to frame land-use regulations and building codes for hilly areas. For example, Uttarakhand and Himachal Pradesh have incorporated LHZ maps into district-level planning. Municipal authorities in Darjeeling, Nilgiris, and Kerala rely on zonation studies to guide urban expansion. By highlighting high-risk corridors, such as the Rishikesh-Badrinath and Siliguri-Gangtok highways, LHZ mapping contributes directly to reducing exposure and preventing recurrent disasters.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Risk analysis extends beyond hazard zonation by incorporating elements of exposure and vulnerability. Hazard indicates the likelihood of landslide occurrence, but risk is determined by the consequences to people, property, infrastructure, and the environment. Vulnerability assessment considers physical vulnerability of structures, socio-economic vulnerability of communities, and environmental sensitivity. Poor households, elderly populations, and marginalized groups are more vulnerable because they often occupy unsafe slopes and lack resources for relocation or mitigation. Risk analysis combines hazard maps with data on population density, infrastructure networks, and economic assets to produce risk zonation maps. These maps are crucial for prioritizing mitigation projects, evacuation planning, and disaster preparedness. In India, pilot studies integrating hazard, vulnerability, and risk are being carried out in Uttarakhand, Himachal, Sikkim, and the northeast, providing models for national application.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Despite progress, several challenges hinder the effectiveness of LHZ mapping in India. Many regions still lack updated hazard maps, or existing maps are at scales too coarse for practical application. Methodologies are not standardized across agencies, leading to inconsistencies. Technical capacity and resources vary widely between states. Integration of hazard maps into land-use planning and enforcement remains weak due to institutional gaps and lack of awareness among local authorities. Future directions include the adoption of uniform national guidelines for LHZ, expanding coverage with high-resolution remote sensing, integrating real-time monitoring data with hazard maps, and linking LHZ directly with climate change adaptation frameworks. Community participation in mapping and validation is also recommended to ensure that maps reflect local knowledge and are used in decision-making. Strengthening partnerships between GSI, state governments, academic institutions, and international agencies will be key to advancing LHZ in India.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Risk treatment is the ultimate aim of landslide risk management and provides the tactics for mitigating hazard effects. Once risk has been analyzed, strategies are selected to minimize it through options such as accepting the risk, avoiding the hazard, reducing likelihood, reducing consequences, installing monitoring and warning systems, transferring risk through insurance, or postponing decisions where data is uncertain. Costs and benefits must be compared to identify economic but effective solutions. Often, combining options provides larger reductions at smaller cost. Each option requires a treatment plan defining responsibilities, work scope, costs, timeframes, performance indicators, and expected outcomes. Continuous monitoring of plans and risks ensures that treatment remains effective as circumstances evolve. Monitoring results also provide feedback for reassessment and refinement. In practice, five broad approaches are used individually or in combination to mitigate landslide risk: restricting development in hazardous zones, adopting excavation and grading codes, protecting existing developments, installing monitoring and warning systems, and using insurance and compensation schemes. Land-use planning is highlighted as one of the most economical and effective strategies, but India lacks uniform procedures for regulating development in unstable areas. Similarly, excavation and grading codes common in other countries are absent in India. Protection of existing developments relies heavily on slope stabilization, drainage, buttresses, retaining walls, piles, caissons, rock anchors, and soil nailing, often used together. Monitoring and warning systems cannot prevent landslides but can issue timely alerts, allowing preventive or emergency action. Insurance and compensation mechanisms are proposed as logical tools to distribute risk and provide incentives for mitigation, but these require legal and institutional reforms in India.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Structural measures for mitigation focus on engineering solutions that stabilize slopes and protect critical structures. Techniques include earth buttresses at slope toes, removal of unstable material, construction of retaining and restraining walls, installation of piles, caissons, anchors, and soil reinforcement. Shear keys, sub-drains, soil nails, and engineered retaining walls are commonly adopted. Drainage correction is emphasized as the most important measure since water infiltration during heavy rainfall increases pore pressure and triggers mass movements. Effective drainage requires maintenance of natural micro- and macro-drainage channels on vulnerable slopes, installation of sub-surface drains, and ensuring unobstructed runoff. These measures reduce infiltration, lower pore water pressures, and improve slope stability, thereby preventing initiation of landslides.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Non-structural measures address landslide risk through policy, ecological, and awareness-based strategies. Proper land-use planning and enforcement of building codes are critical, discouraging new construction in identified hazard-prone areas unless remedial measures are in place. Afforestation programs should be carefully planned to avoid slope modification; selected plant species must withstand stress conditions and provide deep root reinforcement. Awareness generation is equally important: communities must be educated to recognize warning signs of imminent landslides, such as new springs or saturated ground, cracks or bulges in soil and pavements, soil movement away from foundations, sticking doors and windows, broken underground utilities, leaning trees and poles, sunken roadbeds, sudden rises or falls in stream levels, and unusual turbidity in water. Public education empowers individuals to take safety measures, reduces casualties, and fosters a culture of preparedness.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: A critical component of mitigation is the translation of technical hazard information into forms usable by decision-makers and communities. Three elements are emphasized: likelihood of occurrence of a hazardous event; location and extent of its effects on land, structures, and socio-economic activities; and estimated severity of these effects. Communicating these factors in simple terms allows non-technical users to perceive risk and take appropriate action. Preventive mitigation is encouraged, such as planting ground cover on slopes to reduce erosion, installing flexible pipelines to prevent leakage, and reinforcing buildings in hazard zones. Investment in low-cost preventive steps today reduces the impact of future landslides and mudflows. Local emergency management offices are identified as important channels for promoting these practices.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Specific techniques for reducing landslide hazards are grouped into three categories: discouraging development in hazardous areas, removing or converting unsafe developments, and protecting existing developments. Discouraging development involves disclosure of hazards to property buyers, posting warnings, public records, adopting service-area restrictions for utilities, educating the public, and zoning ordinances that reflect hazard levels. Removing or converting development includes acquiring hazardous properties, discouraging non-conforming land uses, reconstructing after landslides, removing unsafe structures, and clearing or redeveloping blighted zones. Financial incentives, insurance requirements, and clarifying liability of property owners are also key tools. Protecting existing development requires engineering measures to control landslides, slumps, mudflows, debris flows, and rockfalls. Monitoring, warning, and evacuation systems are critical, supported by emergency response plans and facility-specific evacuation strategies. Together, these structural, non-structural, financial, and emergency measures create a comprehensive framework for reducing landslide losses.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Capacity building is a cornerstone of landslide risk reduction. Landslide disasters cannot be prevented by engineering solutions alone; they require strong institutions, skilled professionals, empowered communities, and informed individuals. Building capacity involves creating awareness of hazard risks, developing technical skills for hazard mapping and mitigation, and ensuring institutional readiness to respond. Local governments, district authorities, engineers, planners, and community organizations all need training in identifying risk zones, enforcing land-use regulations, monitoring slopes, and organizing preparedness drills. A critical part of capacity building is bridging the knowledge gap between scientists and communities, ensuring that research findings are translated into usable formats for local decision-making. This not only reduces vulnerability but also enhances resilience by empowering people to act as the first line of defense against landslides.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Institutional capacity is essential to ensure that landslide risk management becomes part of governance. The National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM) plays a nodal role by designing training programmes, publishing guidelines, and building a national cadre of trainers and experts. State Disaster Management Authorities (SDMAs) and district authorities are responsible for implementing guidelines at the regional level. Universities and research institutions contribute by advancing scientific methods for hazard zonation, early warning, and slope stabilization. Education curricula at schools and colleges should integrate disaster risk reduction modules so that the culture of safety becomes part of society from an early stage. Professional training for engineers, planners, and administrators ensures that infrastructure projects, road alignments, housing layouts, and urban expansion plans in hilly areas are informed by landslide risk assessments.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Community participation is vital for effective landslide risk management. Local residents are often the first to notice warning signs of instability such as new cracks, tilting poles, or unusual water seepage. Harnessing community knowledge allows early reporting of hazards and timely evacuation. Participatory approaches in hazard mapping, monitoring, and decision-making create ownership of mitigation strategies. Community-based disaster management committees can organize mock drills, establish evacuation routes, and disseminate hazard warnings through local communication networks. Awareness campaigns and training in schools, women’s groups, and farmers’ associations build resilience at the grassroots. Experience shows that communities with strong local organizations and awareness suffer fewer casualties and recover faster after disasters. Empowering people through training, micro-level planning, and social networks ensures that landslide management is not just a top-down exercise but a shared responsibility.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Landslide risk management demands interdisciplinary and cross-sectoral partnerships. Geologists, engineers, ecologists, hydrologists, urban planners, and social scientists must work together to create sustainable solutions. Partnerships between government agencies, NGOs, research institutes, and international organizations expand technical resources and foster innovation. For example, collaboration with meteorological agencies enhances rainfall-based early warning, while partnerships with universities improve geotechnical investigations. NGOs and community-based organizations play a key role in mobilizing public participation and awareness. Cross-sectoral collaboration also ensures that landslide risk reduction is mainstreamed into development, climate adaptation, forestry, and watershed management policies. By aligning institutional mandates and community needs, partnerships strengthen both scientific credibility and social acceptance of landslide mitigation strategies.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Early Warning Systems (EWS) are designed to reduce loss of life and damage by providing timely alerts of impending slope failures. Unlike earthquakes, which strike without notice, landslides often show precursory signs such as ground cracks, soil creep, abnormal water seepage, and bulging slopes. These signs, combined with rainfall thresholds and geotechnical monitoring, allow forecasting of slope instability. EWS integrate hazard detection, risk communication, and community response. They do not prevent landslides, but they enable evacuation, emergency measures, and temporary closure of transport corridors. In India, where hilly terrain is densely inhabited, EWS are essential for highways, pilgrim routes, hydropower projects, and urban settlements in unstable zones. The success of an EWS depends not only on technical accuracy but also on timely dissemination and community trust in the alerts.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: A wide range of monitoring techniques are used to support early warning for landslides. Field observation by trained personnel remains the simplest and often most effective method, especially in rural areas. Modern systems use geotechnical and geophysical instruments to measure slope movements and triggering factors. Inclinometers track subsurface deformation, while extensometers measure surface displacement. Piezometers monitor pore water pressure, and rain gauges provide rainfall intensity data. Ground vibration meters and trip wires detect sudden slope movements. Remote sensing technologies such as LiDAR, InSAR, and satellite optical imagery track terrain deformation over large areas. Radar and laser beams provide real-time monitoring of slope displacements. Data from these instruments can be telemetered to central stations for automated alerts. Integration of multiple instruments enhances reliability, as rainfall, hydrology, and slope deformation are often interlinked in triggering landslides.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Rainfall is the most common trigger of landslides, making rainfall thresholds a key component of early warning systems. Thresholds are defined as critical levels of cumulative rainfall or rainfall intensity that historically precede slope failures in a given area. For example, 100 mm of rainfall within 24 hours may serve as a warning threshold in one region, while 300 mm over 72 hours may be necessary in another, depending on soil type, slope, and vegetation. Predictive models combine rainfall data with soil moisture, groundwater levels, and slope stability calculations to forecast failures. In India, pilot projects in Uttarakhand, Darjeeling, and the northeast are experimenting with rainfall-based thresholds, supported by automatic rain gauge networks. Integration with weather forecasts allows anticipation of hazard events several days in advance. Threshold-based systems require continuous calibration using real event data to maintain reliability.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Community-based early warning is essential to ensure alerts translate into action. Technical monitoring must be paired with communication systems that reach vulnerable populations quickly. Local disaster management committees, NGOs, and panchayats can play a central role in disseminating warnings through sirens, loudspeakers, SMS alerts, radio broadcasts, and local networks. In remote Himalayan and northeastern villages, traditional communication channels such as temple bells or community messengers are still effective. Training communities to recognize precursory signs such as new ground cracks, leaning poles, or sudden changes in stream flow empowers them to issue localized alerts. Participation in monitoring builds trust, ensuring that warnings are taken seriously. Effective EWS include feedback mechanisms where communities report observed changes, contributing to the refinement of technical models.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Early warning systems for landslides must be integrated into institutional frameworks to be effective. In India, the Geological Survey of India (GSI), India Meteorological Department (IMD), state disaster management authorities, and local administrations must work together to link hazard detection with response protocols. Highways, railways, and hydropower projects in landslide-prone areas should maintain dedicated monitoring units linked to district control rooms. Integration with national weather forecasting and satellite monitoring can provide multi-hazard early warning platforms. Future directions include the use of AI-based predictive models, IoT-enabled sensors, and crowdsourced reporting from mobile apps. Climate change adaptation strategies should incorporate landslide EWS, as more intense rainfall events are expected. Institutionalizing early warning in disaster management plans at state and district levels will ensure sustainability, coordination, and mainstreaming of landslide risk reduction.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: A comprehensive landslide risk management strategy combines pre-disaster prevention and post-disaster response. Pre-disaster strategies include hazard assessment, documentation of past events, hazard zonation mapping, and application of modern geotechnical and monitoring techniques to prevent activation of slope failures. Hazard zonation helps planners avoid locating settlements or infrastructure in vulnerable areas while prescribing appropriate slope treatment. Common remediation practices include slope geometry correction, toe protection using retaining structures, management of surface and subsurface water to control pore pressures, and reinforcing technologies such as soil nailing, bolting, anchoring, micropiling, geogrids, geotextiles, and afforestation. Most landslides in India occur during the monsoon due to elevated pore pressures, but factors such as toe erosion by rivers and high-velocity stream discharge from slope crowns also contribute to debris flows. Surface and subsurface water management, especially through drainage networks, is considered the most effective remediation measure, but subsurface drainage remains underutilized in India. Agencies like the Border Roads Organisation and state PWDs need to adopt modern technologies for drainage construction and slope stabilization. Reinforcement technologies such as nailing, bolting, and tie-backs have been successfully applied to stabilize slopes in road cuttings, open cast mines, tunnels, and landslide-prone areas, though India still lacks innovation in developing site-specific solutions. Detailed geological and geotechnical investigations are essential before stabilisation works, since slope morphology and causes of failure must be properly understood to design effective remediation. Stabilisation methods depend on risk, cost-effectiveness, and site-specific conditions, involving modifications of topography, geology, groundwater, and related factors.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Drainage control is a primary method for slope remediation. Surface drainage works reduce infiltration of rainwater that builds pore pressure. These include catchwater drains above landslide crowns, contour drains across slide masses, and cascading chute drains to intercept and divert rainfall. Subsurface drainage removes groundwater within slide masses using shallow or deep techniques such as interceptor trench drains, horizontal gravity drains, underdrains, drainage wells, and drainage tunnels. Soil and debris removal is another effective treatment, especially for small to medium landslides. This involves offloading unstable slide material from the crown downwards and creating benches or berms to stabilize slopes. Buttress fills act as counterweights placed at the toe of a slide to improve stability, often using soil removed from the crown. River training and erosion control measures prevent toe erosion that destabilizes slopes, using check dams, bank protection, and deflection spurs upstream of vulnerable slopes. These measures help counteract the scouring effects of high-velocity flows that often initiate slide activity.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Restraining structures provide direct stabilization of sliding masses. These include pile works that anchor unstable slides into firm ground, anchor and bolt systems that use tensile resistance across unstable zones, and retaining or breast walls that prevent secondary slides along slope toes. After remediation measures are applied, treated slopes must be protected from atmospheric agents like rainfall and snow. Protective coverings include afforestation to create vegetative root networks, slope bioengineering using live plant materials that strengthen over time, and vegetation designed to intercept precipitation, reduce runoff, and limit infiltration. Shotcreting with or without wire-mesh is widely used to protect slopes of weathered rock, often combined with drainage holes. Covering slope surfaces with natural or synthetic geo-fabrics also reduces weathering and erosion. Biotechnical methods, pioneered in Europe and adapted in India, combine aggressive planting with structural supports to provide long-term slope stabilization. These methods not only shield slopes but also enhance shear strength, ecological recovery, and sustainable stabilization of hilly terrain.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Stabilization of hill slopes can be achieved by improving the mechanical characteristics of unstable ground using two main approaches: insertion of reinforcement elements and improvement of soil strength through treatments. Reinforcement techniques include installation of large diameter wells, crowns of consolidated earth columns, networks of micropiles, anchors, soil nailing, and cement or chemical grouting depending on material properties. Ground improvement can also involve thermal treatment of clayey slopes, electro-osmotic treatment of homogeneous clay soils, or mechanical compaction methods. These methods alter soil properties, increase shear strength, and reduce pore water pressure, making slopes more stable. Reinforcement technologies have found wide applications in India for problematic slopes, open cast mines, tunnels, and road cuttings, but require site-specific design and geotechnical investigation.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Mitigation of slides and slumps includes drainage, excavation, restraining structures, vegetation, and soil hardening. Drainage is most effective as groundwater is the main contributor to instability. Surface drainage involves ditches, regrading, and sealing cracks to prevent infiltration, while subsurface methods include horizontal drains, wells, trench drains, galleries, blanket drains, electro-osmosis, blasting, and barriers. Excavation techniques involve removing slide masses, unloading upper slope sections, regrading slopes, and replacing toe materials. Restraining structures include retaining walls, piles, buttresses, tie rods, anchors, and rock bolts. Vegetation stabilizes soil through root reinforcement and interception of rainfall. Soil hardening involves chemical treatment, freezing, thermal treatment, and grouting to increase cohesion. Site levelling smooths slope surfaces, sealing cracks and depressions to prevent ponding and infiltration. Drainpipes, especially horizontal slotted PVC pipes, are widely used in highway construction to drain slopes. Straw wattles and straw bales provide temporary protection against erosion and runoff. Retaining wall designs range from timber crib, steel bin, reinforced earth, and gabion walls, each with unique construction requirements and stability mechanisms. Pile walls provide resistance at slope toes, though their effectiveness depends on soil volume and depth of failure surfaces.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Debris-flow hazards are mitigated through source-area stabilization, energy dissipation, flow control, and direct protection. Source measures include check dams and revegetation. Energy dissipation and flow control involve deflection walls, debris basins, fences, deflection dams, and channelization. Direct protection uses impact-spreading walls, stem walls, and vegetation barriers. Check dams are sediment-storage structures built in gullies to stabilize channels, control travelling, and store debris. They can be constructed of reinforced concrete or log cribs, spaced according to gradient and dam height. Failures can be prevented by tying wing walls into canyon walls and providing stable foundations. Debris basins built at slope bases capture flows but require periodic removal of deposits. Debris retaining walls divert or stop flows but must be carefully designed to avoid redirecting hazards into other vulnerable areas. Strengthening slopes against erosion also reduces debris-flow hazards, with measures such as afforestation, fire prevention, and erosion control. Burnt slopes and vegetation loss make areas more prone to debris flows, highlighting the need for integrated erosion and fire management.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Rockfalls range from small stones to massive cliff sections and can travel great distances, endangering settlements, transport corridors, and recreational areas. Stabilization methods include excavation, benching, scaling, trimming, rock bolts, anchors, cables, anchored mesh nets, shotcrete, buttresses, and dentition. Protection methods include rock-trap ditches, catch nets and fences, catch walls, and rock sheds or tunnels built over roads and railways. Excavation and benching reduce tension in surface rocks, intercept rockfall, and reduce erosion, though they are less effective against deep-seated failures. Scaling removes loose blocks using pry bars or explosives, while trimming eliminates larger overhangs. Shotcrete and gunite sprayed onto rock faces reinforce surface blocks and reduce weathering. Anchors, bolts, and dowels tie rock faces together, requiring specialist geotechnical expertise. Catch ditches and gabion walls intercept rocks but must be designed for site-specific geometry. Cable mesh and rock curtains prevent smaller blocks from falling, while catch nets dissipate rockfall energy. Rock sheds and shelters deflect falling masses away from roads and railways, providing protection for critical infrastructure.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Integrated protection systems for rockfalls combine multiple measures for maximum effectiveness. Cable lashing and nets restrain large blocks, while wire mesh prevents small rocks from dislodging. Draped or bolted mesh provides stability to irregular slopes. Rock fences and curtains direct rocks to catch ditches, but require proper installation to prevent bypass. Retaining walls built from steel, concrete, timbers, or gabions stop rockfall debris but must be anchored to avoid collapse. Rock sheds and tunnels provide complete shielding for roads, railways, and facilities. In unique cases, rock ledge reinforcement is applied for localized stabilization. These systems must consider site-specific factors such as slope angle, rock type, fracture patterns, and hydrological conditions. By combining stabilization, protection, and controlled catchment, integrated systems provide long-term safety for human activities in landslide-prone hilly terrain.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Vegetation is a proven method of slope stabilization that prevents erosion and provides long-term reinforcement to soil. Seeding with grasses and legumes reduces surface erosion and limits shallow slope failures, while planting shrubs adds deeper root systems that improve slope stability. If unchecked, small surface failures can evolve into larger landslides, making early vegetation treatment critical. The terms bioengineering and biotechnical slope stabilization refer to using vegetation as slope protection to arrest and prevent failure. Successful revegetation requires careful planning, including local expertise to select plant species and proper timing of seeding to avoid drought or frost. Preparatory measures such as drainage control, cut-bank removal, slope angle reduction, and benching must precede seeding. This integrated approach ensures that vegetation provides not only mechanical root reinforcement but also hydrological benefits by reducing infiltration and enhancing evapotranspiration.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Mulching is a non-living surface treatment that protects soil from erosion and retains moisture. Materials such as straw, grass fibers, wood chips, seaweed, or paper products can be spread over disturbed slopes to reduce rainfall impact and conserve soil moisture until vegetation is established. While mulching is temporary, it is a low-cost and effective erosion control method when immediate protection is required. Biotechnical slope protection builds upon mulching and vegetation by integrating natural and structural measures. Traditional concrete and steel retaining structures are often environmentally intrusive, whereas biotechnical solutions combine soil, vegetation, and lightweight structural elements such as geonets anchored by soil nails or geocells seeded with vegetation. Research has highlighted vetiver grass as particularly effective in stabilizing slopes across diverse environments, offering a practical and inexpensive bio-based solution. These eco-friendly approaches reduce environmental impacts while providing structural and hydrological benefits.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Bio-engineering is the use of live plant materials as structural components for slope stabilization and erosion control. It provides immediate reinforcement through planted stems and longer-term stabilization as adventitious roots develop. Vegetation contributes mechanical reinforcement, surface armoring, hydrological drainage, and soil binding, thereby reducing slope failure risk. The benefits include catching downslope material, limiting erosion, improving shear strength, draining excess water, and supporting regeneration of soil and micro-catchments. Bio-engineering is rarely used in isolation but is integrated with civil measures such as check dams, toe walls, jute netting, and prop walls. Vegetation-based engineering is environmentally sustainable and provides social and economic benefits, especially for rural farmers in hilly regions. By blending roads and slopes into the landscape, bio-engineering helps restore ecosystems, rehabilitate spoil disposal sites, and reduce damage to agriculture, forestry, and horticulture. It enhances resilience, improves long-term sustainability of roads and infrastructure in hills, and represents the most ecological form of slope protection.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Landslide dams pose their greatest hazard through catastrophic flooding, either when water overtops them or when they suddenly breach. Immediate measures are necessary to protect downstream communities. One option is diversion of inflow water before it reaches the lake, channeling it into upstream reservoirs or irrigation systems to slow the filling of the dammed basin. Although temporary, this provides valuable time for planning long-term stabilization. Another method is temporary drainage using pumps or siphons to lower water levels by allowing flow over the dam’s lowest point. These are short-term solutions, usually lasting one to two years, that help prevent rapid overtopping while more durable measures are implemented. The most common approach is construction of erosion-resistant open-channel spillways, either across the dam body or an adjacent abutment. Spillways function like emergency channels on engineered dams, controlling water levels and reducing upstream flooding. They must be wide and shallow to minimize velocity and lined with erosion-resistant materials such as riprap. Check dams can also be installed along steep spillway grades to slow flow and prevent retrogressive erosion. Even when spillways eventually fail, they reduce stored water volume, lowering the severity of downstream flooding if the dam breaches.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: A more permanent method for reducing landslide dam hazards is construction of diversion tunnels through adjacent abutments. These tunnels redirect flow away from the dam, preventing overtopping and catastrophic breaching. Large landslide dams often form in narrow mountain canyons with bedrock walls, making them suitable for rock-tunneling techniques. The landslide dam itself is often left intact due to its massive size, while monitoring systems are installed to detect changes in stability. Excavation of spillways and tunnels requires careful planning because the rugged terrain makes access difficult, often requiring new road construction and the use of bulldozers, draglines, backhoes, explosives, or hand labor. While costly and time-intensive, drainage tunnels provide a long-term safeguard, ensuring controlled water passage and greatly reducing the risk of sudden dam failure and downstream devastation.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: An effective response to landslide disasters requires a prompt, well-coordinated system that minimizes loss of life and property and enables early recovery. The essential ingredients of such a system include integrated institutional arrangements, state-of-the-art forecasting and warning mechanisms, reliable communication networks, rapid evacuation, deployment of specialized response forces, and strong coordination among agencies at all levels. All agencies and their personnel must clearly understand their roles, responsibilities, and actions to be taken in both actual and threatening disaster situations. The objectives of the Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) are threefold: to provide a concise list of executive actions for preparedness, response, and relief; to ensure ministries, departments, and district administrations know their precise measures and coordinate closely; and to indicate state and UT responsibilities for preparing contingency action plans. The SOP covers five phases of disaster management. The Preparedness Phase involves planning, capacity building, resource mobilization, training, and mock drills. The Early Warning Phase ensures timely, qualitative, and quantitative alerts for preemptive action. The Response Phase covers immediate search, rescue, and evacuation operations. The Relief Phase provides essential needs such as food, water, shelter, health, clothing, and hygiene. The Restoration Phase includes measures to stabilize conditions and restore utilities. This SOP focuses only on immediate response and relief measures, not on long-term mitigation, rehabilitation, or reconstruction, which require separate frameworks.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Emergency Operations Centres (EOCs) or control rooms at national, state, and district levels are the nerve centres for disaster coordination. Their objectives include receiving and processing disaster alerts, issuing timely warnings, monitoring operations, coordinating among ministries and agencies, requisitioning resources, issuing incident-specific instructions, consolidating damage and needs assessment data, and forwarding reports to designated authorities. EOCs must be located in disaster-resistant buildings that remain functional during emergencies. They require fail-proof communication networks with triple redundancy using NICNET, POLNET, and SPACENET, in addition to terrestrial and satellite systems for voice, data, and video transfer. State EOCs will be directly connected with the National EOC under MHA, while district EOCs will be linked to their respective states. All must function 24x7, manned by trained personnel capable of operating control systems year-round.

metadata.agency: National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA), NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: India has established the National Disaster Response Force (NDRF) for specialized response to natural and man-made emergencies. NDRF consists of 10 battalions drawn from central paramilitary forces, each with 18 search and rescue teams including doctors, paramedics, and dog squads. NDMA provides overall direction, while command lies with the Director General of Civil Defence and NDRF. At the state level, State Disaster Response Forces (SDRF) are to be raised on the NDRF model, with responsibility for preparedness and capacity building resting with state governments. National Disaster Mitigation Resource Centres (NDMRCs) are co-located with NDRF battalions and stockpile essential relief supplies for the first 72–96 hours after a disaster. These centres support mock drills, capacity development, and coordination of central resources during emergencies. States are required to establish their own State Disaster Mitigation Resource Centres (SDMRCs) for pre-positioning supplies, evaluating capabilities, and projecting requirements. Inter-state cooperation through memoranda of understanding is encouraged to facilitate mutual assistance during major calamities.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Disaster response in India includes multiple support services beyond NDRF and SDRF. Fire and Emergency Services in states and union territories are being upgraded into multi-hazard response units equipped according to local needs. Civil Defence volunteers will be enrolled under the Civil Defence Act and trained at national and state institutions for use in natural disaster response. While the creation of NDRF reduces reliance on the armed forces, the military will still be deployed in situations beyond the capacity of state governments and NDRF. Response functions are organized as Emergency Support Functions (ESFs), each led by a primary ministry or agency responsible for coordinating service delivery with supporting organizations. ESFs form part of EOCs and must coordinate from designated centres, deploying extension teams to disaster sites. States are also required to secure pre-contracts for essential commodities such as medicines, tents, boats, and relief supplies before monsoon and cyclone seasons. These contracts ensure rapid delivery at pre-agreed rates, guaranteeing timely support during emergencies.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: District level preparedness for landslide response involves a systematic approach to local risk management. Each district must conduct vulnerability assessments to identify potential hazards and evaluate both risks and local response capacity. Communities should be informed of their vulnerabilities through Panchayati Raj Institutions, local governments, and NGOs to build awareness and readiness. A comprehensive directory of officials engaged in disaster management at national, state, and district levels must be compiled, including their names, contact numbers, email addresses, and official designations. This directory should be widely distributed and regularly updated. Preparedness must also be subject to annual reviews at national, state, district, and sub-divisional levels to ensure response systems remain efficient, effective, and timely. Reviews will include reassessment of resources, preparedness levels, and the availability of essential commodities for response and relief. Decentralization of authority is highlighted as a key feature, empowering relief commissioners and district magistrates to act quickly. Mock drills play a crucial role in testing preparedness. National and state search and rescue teams are required to conduct annual drills for various hazards, with cyclone drills in March and September, flood drills in June, and earthquake and landslide drills typically in March. District-level mock exercises ensure manpower, equipment, and procedures function effectively under simulated disaster conditions.

metadata.agency: National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM), Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: The India Disaster Resource Network (IDRN) is a web-enabled national inventory of resources for disaster management. State governments are responsible for ensuring that designated district authorities regularly enter and update data, at least once a month, so that resources are always visible during emergencies. The National Crisis Management Committee (NCMC), chaired by the Cabinet Secretary, is activated during disasters to take stock of the situation, and issue necessary directions to the NEC, MHA, central ministries, agencies, and state governments. The National Executive Committee (NEC), convened under the Union Home Secretary, assesses the situation and provides operational directions to central and state authorities, coordinates the response of multiple agencies, deputes official teams for on-site assessment, mobilizes resources, and continuously monitors and reviews disaster situations. Nodal and other central ministries play a critical role during response by deputing officials to the NEOC, coordinating with counterparts, assessing immediate needs in their sectors, and mobilizing resources and manpower to assist affected states.

metadata.agency: State Disaster Management Authorities (SDMAs), NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: At the state level, the State Emergency Operation Centre (SEOC) is activated upon receiving information from NEOC, DEOC, early warning agencies, or other reliable sources. Its functions include issuing alerts and warnings to authorities and the public through AIR, Doordarshan, and press; sending first information reports to NEOC and MHA followed by daily situation reports; collecting and analyzing information; arranging State Executive Committee (SEC) meetings; and activating state emergency support functions if required. The State Disaster Management Authority (SDMA), chaired by the Chief Minister, reviews the situation and issues directions to the SEC, Department of Disaster Management, and state agencies. It also decides on inter-state cooperation. The State Executive Committee (SEC), convened by the Secretary of Disaster Management, directs departments and DDMAs on response measures, coordinates multi-agency responses, mobilizes resources, and may request NDRF or armed forces if necessary. The SEC supervises relief operations, monitors situations regularly, informs NEC and NDMA, and projects anticipated requirements for central resources. In severe cases, it may appoint senior officers with delegated powers for specific regions. State departments and agencies must depute officials to SEOC during activation, coordinate with national counterparts, and mobilize specialized manpower, equipment, and materials to meet needs in their respective sectors.

metadata.agency: District Disaster Management Authorities (DDMAs), NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: At the district level, the District Emergency Operation Centre (DEOC) is fully activated upon receipt of information from SEOC, NEOC, early warning agencies, or field reports from subdivisions and tehsils. DEOC issues alerts and warnings, sends first information and daily situation reports to SEOC, NEOC, and MHA, collects and relays information to decision makers, maintains all disaster records, and activates district-level ESFs when necessary. The District Disaster Management Authority (DDMA) assesses the situation and directs line departments and agencies to undertake response measures. Its functions include coordinating immediate relief, assessing resource capacities, pooling manpower and equipment from different agencies, requisitioning NDRF, armed forces, or specialized support if required, and engaging civil society and NGOs to supplement government efforts. DDMA also monitors and reviews disaster response regularly, ensuring restoration of critical infrastructure and continuity of relief. The decentralization of responsibility at the district level ensures a faster, coordinated response tailored to local conditions.

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: In landslide disasters where no early warning signals are available, community members themselves act as the first responders. Immediate support comes from police, State Disaster Response Forces, fire services, and medical services. Additional responders include Civil Defence, Home Guards, and youth organizations such as NCC, NSS, and NYKS operating through local units. The District Emergency Operation Centre (DEOC) must send a First Information Report within 24 hours of the disaster to the SEOC, NEOC, and Ministry of Home Affairs. This FIR provides a brief account of the severity of the disaster, actions being taken locally, available coping capacities and resources, immediate external relief requirements with approximate quantities, logistics for delivering relief, and a forecast of possible new risks. Daily situation reports are standardized for district, state, and national levels, with states required to send reports every six hours during the first three days and then daily until conditions normalize. For inaccessible areas, airdropping of food and essential commodities is coordinated between state or district authorities and the Indian Air Force through NEC and MHA. Food packets for airdropping, including their contents and quantities, are determined and communicated in advance by district administration to ensure timely and effective delivery.

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Rescue and relief operations following a landslide must be guided by rapid ground assessment of damage and losses. Preliminary assessments are to be completed within 24 hours using teams of officials from multiple sectors, conducting on-site visits, aerial surveys, and collecting information from primary and secondary sources. Immediate repair of essential infrastructure such as electricity, drinking water, and telecommunications is the responsibility of state departments and agencies, to ensure smooth rescue and relief operations. State and district authorities must designate officials responsible for disposal of dead bodies in the event of mass casualties, prioritizing identification and return to next of kin and reserving mass burials or cremations as a last resort, while respecting religious and cultural practices. Similarly, animal carcasses must be disposed of by designated authorities under guidance from the Department of Animal Husbandry. Accurate and timely dissemination of information is critical; press briefings must be conducted regularly by the District Magistrate or Collector, or their authorized representative, to provide a single official source of information to the media. Relief efforts must prioritize the safety, security, and dignity of affected populations, ensuring access to food, water, shelter, clothing, and medical care. Minimum standards of relief will be set by the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) and State Disaster Management Authorities (SDMAs) under relevant statutory provisions.

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Disasters often deprive people of food and immediate food aid must be provided to sustain life. Relief measures include free food distribution to those in greatest need, with distributions discontinued as soon as conditions stabilize. Dry rations are preferable so households can cook, while community kitchens may be organized for short periods when affected populations cannot cook for themselves. Food practices of local communities should be respected, and commodities chosen in consultation with the affected population to ensure appropriateness and acceptability. All food must be of good quality, safe to consume, and nutritionally adequate. General rations should bridge the gap between household food resources and actual needs. Food must be stored, prepared, and consumed safely at both household and community levels, and distribution must be transparent, responsive, and equitable. Government agencies should involve NGOs, community-based organizations, and social organizations to supplement their efforts. Nutritional needs of the population must be met, and specific attention given to at-risk groups to address malnutrition and micronutrient deficiencies.

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Natural disasters frequently disrupt water supplies, leaving affected populations without safe drinking water. State governments and district authorities must identify alternative water sources and arrange timely supply for disaster-hit communities. Adequate facilities must be ensured so that people can collect, store, and use sufficient water for drinking, cooking, and hygiene. Drinking water provided must conform to prescribed quality standards to prevent disease outbreaks. Additionally, water supplied for personal and domestic hygiene must be safe and not create health risks. Ensuring reliable access to safe water is essential to protect health and enable recovery in disaster-affected areas.

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: After a disaster, health risks rise sharply due to poverty, insecurity, overcrowding, and disruption of essential services. Factors such as inadequate quantity and quality of water, poor sanitation, insufficient shelter, and food shortages contribute to outbreaks of diseases and epidemics. Post-disaster environments create conditions that amplify both communicable and non-communicable health threats, making surveillance, quick action, and provision of safe living conditions critical to public health management.

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Medical response in disasters must be rapid and effective. States and Districts are tasked with establishing mechanisms to identify health threats quickly and report them through proper surveillance systems. Key measures include assessing health and nutritional status of affected populations, deploying nearby medical resources across administrative boundaries, and mobilizing mobile medical hospitals from central government reserves. Adequate supply of medicines and disinfectants must be ensured. Vaccination campaigns, especially for children and pregnant women, and inoculation where necessary, are essential. Preventive actions must address water-borne and vector-borne diseases, which often surge after disasters. Timely medical intervention is critical to reducing mortality and morbidity in affected communities.

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Disasters create severe mental trauma for survivors, making psychosocial support and mental health services critical. States and UTs must designate a Nodal Mental Health Officer in each affected district. Rapid needs assessments for psychosocial care must be conducted, with trained manpower mobilized to deliver psychosocial first aid and transfer critical cases to a referral hospitals. Community-level workers, relief staff, and NGOs/CBOs should be trained and sensitized to cultural and ethical practices when providing psychosocial support. Relief camps must integrate mental health services, and long-term referral systems should be established for survivors needing extended care. Preventive practices, including community prayers, religious discourse, and culturally appropriate group support, are recommended to promote resilience and mental well-being in disaster-hit populations.

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: During disasters, affected populations often lose clothing and utensils, essential for dignity and daily living. State and District authorities are mandated to provide sufficient clothing, blankets, and household essentials. Each household must receive basic cooking and eating utensils to ensure survival and maintain personal dignity during relief operations.

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: In major disasters, shelter is a critical survival factor. State/District authorities must provide adequate covered space for those whose homes are destroyed or rendered uninhabitable. Shelters must include artificial lighting for personal security, and households must be supported with tools, equipment, and materials for repair and reconstruction. Shelter provision is linked to human dignity, family cohesion, and resilience in disaster conditions.

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Relief camps must be established in adequate buildings or open spaces, avoiding use of educational institutions. Each camp must be managed by trained members of the Incident Command Team. Preparations include detailed logistics planning, pre-disaster identification of supply agencies, and ensuring provision of drinking water, sanitation, bathing, and health-care facilities. Camps must also have adequate security by police, lighting at common areas, and community participation through task forces from affected families. Governance tools such as entitlement cards and bank accounts for cash transfers should be developed. Sanitation and hygiene are vital to prevent epidemics: distribution of soaps, detergents, sanitary napkins, and provision of safe toilets in camps must ensure health, dignity, and well-being of the displaced population.

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: For devastating disasters with prolonged displacement, intermediate shelters with sanitary facilities must be built to provide a reasonable quality of life. Relief supply management requires robust supply chain protocols covering procurement, packaging, transportation, storage, and distribution of materials. Systems must be established for donations in cash or kind and their equitable distribution. Transparency is mandated—affected people must be informed of their entitlements, receipts for relief materials must be acknowledged, and clear records maintained by SDMAs and DDMAs to ensure accountability.

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Geological Survey of India (GSI) issues alerts and warnings to designated authorities and agencies of Central and State Governments for landslides. These alerts are categorized into four levels based on scale, location, and potential impact. The categories provide early warnings to enable preparedness and response actions by disaster management authorities. Category I (Red): Landslides of large dimensions located in or near inhabited areas such as urban or large rural settlements. These can cause significant human casualties and large-scale infrastructure damage. They may also block busy pilgrimage routes, disrupt transport, and create large landslide dams. Sudden dam breaches can release massive floodwaters, causing catastrophic flash floods and destruction downstream. Category II (Orange): Landslides occurring on the fringes of inhabited areas, potentially resulting in limited loss of life and property. These may block small natural drainages and create smaller reservoirs, posing delayed but real threats. Though impacts may not be immediate, they still require attention and preparedness. Category III (Orange): Landslides of fairly large size that affect key infrastructure such as highways, rail routes, hydroelectric and irrigation projects. These events may also enter large water bodies, including reservoirs of hydroelectric projects, causing potential damage to critical installations. Category IV (Yellow): Landslides of small dimensions that occur away from habitations. These generally do not affect people or their possessions, though they may temporarily block roads, rail routes, or communication lines. Their impact is localized and not significant to society as a whole.”

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Rapid Damage and Loss Assessment is critical for effective disaster response, focusing on determining the extent of harm to life, property, infrastructure, economy, and environment. The objective is to identify precise nature and extent of damage so that rescue and relief measures can be prioritized. Key components include: (i) geographical area impacted, (ii) structural damage to housing and establishments, (iii) damage to roads, bridges, public buildings, shops and workshops, (iv) damage to utilities like water supply, electricity, public services, and (v) loss to agricultural crops and livestock. Tools for rapid assessment include aerial surveys, photographs, video/films, satellite imagery, field reports, media coverage, visual inspection checklists, and technical equipment such as camera, laptop, GIS map, GPS, compass, and charging apparatus.”

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Search and Rescue (SAR) operations aim to locate and save live victims trapped during landslides. A SAR squad typically consists of a squad leader and five or more rescuers. The squad leader prepares the search plan, diagrams, and documentation, while rescuers carry out the physical search. Basic tools include probing radar, radios, walkie-talkies, site marking supplies, flags, cones, and communication devices. Warning and alert devices include megaphones, whistles, hammers, and horns. Reconnaissance tools include binoculars, cameras, flashlights, and technical search diagrams. The search process involves compiling available information, securing the scene, inspecting and evaluating the site, rescuing victims with easy access, selecting search areas and tools, applying search patterns, and providing pre-hospital treatment. Search Modalities include two primary types: (1) Hasty Search or Primary Search, which rapidly detects surface victims, sets priorities, and provides initial life-saving response. (2) Extensive or Grid Search, which is systematic, covers the assigned area thoroughly, provides redundant checks, and pinpoints victim locations. Both methods complement each other, with hasty search offering speed and extensive search offering completeness.
