Search Methods for landslide response include: (i) Physical Search – using human senses and simple procedures, often the first and only method in local communities without specialized resources; (ii) Canine Search – utilizing specially trained dogs to detect live victims quickly, effective in unstable or inaccessible areas, and able to cover large areas in short time; (iii) Technical Search – performed by highly trained personnel using advanced equipment to detect sound, vibration, temperature, and video signals to locate survivors. Coordination between physical, canine, and technical methods maximizes effectiveness of SAR operations.”

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Basic rescue skills include knot tying, emergency casualty handling, and various carrying methods. Knot tying is essential in rescue work since lives may depend on secure knots tied under any condition. Important knots are simple figure eight, reef knot, clove hitch, figure eight-on-a-eight, sheet bend, bowline, round turn and two half hitches, and timber hitch. Natural fibre rope should not be used for human rescue, as it may weaken in the sun, though it can suffice for other tasks.

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Emergency handling of casualties prioritizes arranging medical help quickly and rescuing injured people from danger. In mass casualty situations, triage is applied to rank victims by urgency of first aid and transport. Rescuers must stay calm, avoid hazards, and act quickly. For suspected head or spinal injuries, movement should be minimized unless absolutely necessary, maintaining anatomical alignment. The first step is to assess the number of casualties, check responsiveness, breathing, and control severe bleeding. Time should not be wasted on minor injuries until all casualties are stabilized. One-rescuer methods emphasize minimal movement and safe escape routes. If alone, rescuers can use techniques such as human crutch, drag carry, blanket drag, pick-a-back, removal downstairs, or firefighter’s crawl. The principle is to move casualties only as far as necessary for safety. Two-rescuer methods provide safer options to carry casualties and include chair lift, two-hand seat carry, or four-hand seat carry methods. These techniques distribute weight between two rescuers, allowing for more stability and reduced strain. Multi-rescuer methods are used when more than two people are available to move casualties. Options include blanket lift, three-person lift and carry, improvised stretchers, blanket and pole stretchers, stretchers from found materials, blanketing stretchers, lashing casualty to stretcher, and coordinated stretcher carrying techniques. These methods are most effective for heavy or severely injured victims requiring stable transport.

metadata.agency: NIDM, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India

chunk: Five stages of rescue are generally followed by trained rescue parties. Stage 1 focuses on reconnaissance and dealing with surface casualties. Rescuers examine the site, address casualties visible on the surface, and collect all available information about other potential occupants of the building. Stage 2 involves locating and removing lightly-trapped casualties. Rescuers conduct immediate searches in accessible areas to rescue people with minimal effort. Contact should be maintained with visible or audible casualties who cannot be moved immediately. Trained air-scenting dogs are highly effective in this stage for locating conscious or unconscious victims, though handlers must ensure dogs are not hindered by collars or harnesses and check paws regularly for injuries. Stage 3 focuses on exploration of likely survival points. Rescuers search the ruins, calling and listening, to identify and rescue all persons who can be seen or heard within debris. Stage 4 emphasizes further exploration and selected debris removal. Rescuers extend searches deeper into ruins, even in areas with remote chances of survival, focusing on removing debris from locations where casualties are most likely to be trapped. Stage 5 involves systematic debris removal. Selected areas are stripped of debris until all casualties are accounted for, including recovery of dead bodies and body parts. Buildings that have been searched are to be clearly marked with spray paint or signs, which can also indicate the presence of bodies.

Some Do’s and Don’ts: Rescue workers must be able to perform critical skills even in difficult conditions such as darkness or cramped spaces. Knot-tying should be practiced blindfolded. Before emergencies, volunteers should engage with municipal emergency plans, prepare family emergency kits, keep emergency contacts, undergo first aid training, and ensure protective gear like gloves, boots, helmets, safety glasses, and masks are ready. During response, workers should think carefully before acting, alert authorities of damages and casualties, conduct reconnaissance before rescue, stay close to walls on damaged stairs or floors, use gloves when handling debris, protect casualties from dust and falling debris with blankets or tarps, minimize disturbance to wreckage, use props or struts to reinforce weak structures, and prioritize life-threatening injuries in triage. Key practices include clearing airways, keeping casualties warm, securing them properly on stretchers, carefully navigating debris, and recording all casualties handled. Don’ts include moving injured persons without rendering first aid unless in immediate danger, smoking or lighting flames near gas leaks, disturbing or collapsing debris unnecessarily, pulling out structural supports indiscriminately, entering dangerous sites alone or without informing others, touching loose electrical wiring, or discarding debris haphazardly.

Safety in Rescue Operation Rescue operations are the most complex and difficult activities that first responder teams face. Numerous factors affect safety during such operations. Every rescuer should follow some basic rules: Personal Protective Equipment must be worn by all entering the area; Hygiene practices such as handwashing before/after eating, using toilets, or entering/leaving work areas must be followed; a Safety Officer must be designated with authority to stop or halt unsafe operations; Group Safety requires team leaders to monitor personnel safety, with whistles as emergency alert tools; Whistle Signals include one long signal (stop and await instructions), one long and one short (continue work), and three short signals (evacuate immediately to safety zone). Safety Zones should be pre-designated for emergencies. Emergency Medical Services should be available within 15 minutes, supported by first aid kits and communications. Drinking Water must be carried to prevent dehydration. Team safety requires operations with tools and equipment to be done in pairs, one acting as operator and the other as safety lookout, using shoulder-tap signals (one tap stop, two taps continue). The Rescue Team Leader is ultimately responsible for team safety. If no Safety Officer is designated, each member must remain alert and prevent unsafe actions during all phases of operations.

Victim Management Safety is everyone’s responsibility. The following concepts and procedures should be applied starting from the moment the search is initiated until the last victim is found. Precautions during a search include: never making inappropriate comments and always maintaining a positive tone, as victims may be listening; assuming victims are in the worst possible position and struggling to survive, where your positive attitude may enhance their chances of survival; and projecting confidence and hope when communicating, as you may be the first person the victim speaks to. Steps for initial contact with a located victim include: overcoming language barriers, identifying yourself clearly as a rescuer with confidence and calmness, and obtaining key information such as name, whether they are an adult or child, type and extent of injury, hydration status, warmth, and degree of confinement. Emergency medical help should be provided as quickly as possible. Rescuers should also ask about other potential victims, inform the located victim of ongoing rescue operations, notify them if leaving temporarily, and provide protection from the environment. When possible, direct or indirect interventions from a relative or friend should be considered to support the victim.

First Aid is the temporary help given to an injured or sick person before professional medical treatment. It may be termed as emergency care offered to a victim of sudden illness or injury until more skillful help is available. This timely assistance, comprising of simple medical techniques, is critical and can often save life and improve vital signs. Any layperson can be trained to administer first aid with minimal equipment. Training should be taught in schools, workplaces, and widely in society. Equally important is to know what not to do in such situations.Categories of First Aid: Emergency first aid: first positive response to life-threatening medical emergencies. Includes CPR, clearing airway obstruction, treating anaphylactic shock, splinting fractures, severe bleeding control.Non-emergency first aid: treatment for minor medical needs such as minor bleeding, cuts, abrasions, or pain management.Initial Steps when Reaching a Victim: Assess the injured: Is the person alive? Can they be removed? Quick triage to prioritize those most likely to survive. Lifting and extraction: Move only if safe. Let injured arm hang free, stabilize neck, lift carefully. Assess pulse, skin color, level of consciousness (AVPU: Alert, Verbal, Pain, Unresponsive).ABCDE for Seriously Injured:A – Airways: clear obstructions, perform HEIMLICH if needed, lift chin, avoid unnecessary neck tilting.B – Breathing: ensure breathing, check ribs, close chest wounds, provide mouth-to-mouth.C – Circulation: check pulse and bleeding. Control bleeding with direct pressure, elevation, pressure points. Avoid tourniquets. Manage shock with head down and legs raised. Do not remove embedded sharp objects.D – Disability: assess neurological status, stabilize neck/spine.E – Exposure & Temperature Control: protect from hypo/hyperthermia, suffocation, drowning.Stabilization & Environment Control: Neck/spine stabilization is vital; assume cervical injury in chest/neck trauma. Place victims in neutral, comfortable position. Take utmost care during transfer and reassure the victim. Prevent environmental harm (temperature extremes, suffocation, drowning).Expert Resuscitation: Suction, intubation, oxygen therapy, medication, CPR, defibrillation, bleeding control, stabilization.Transportation: Identify priority cases, arrange appropriate transport and personnel, take to closest or most appropriate center. Always transport in recovery position. Avoid transporting unconscious patients on their back without airway care (a major cause of preventable death).First Aid for Fractures: Spine: move with extreme care, use multiple people, support with rolled towel as collar, strap to plank. Pelvis: triangular bandage, pad between thighs. Arms: sling and strap to body. Legs: splint with bamboo or tie to the other leg.Survival Skills: Avoid hazards, prevent further injury, escape safely, communicate, and do not endanger yourself. If conscious: stay calm, breathe normally, move minimally, conserve energy, control bleeding with compression.Key Skills to Learn: Recovery position placement. Vessel compression. HEIMLICH maneuver. Mouth-to-mouth artificial respiration and CPR.

metadata.agency: Government of India, Ministry of Home Affairs, NDMA, Finance Commission

chunk: Relief describes the financial and institutional mechanisms for disaster management as recommended by the 13th Finance Commission for the period 2010 to 2015. The Commission reviewed the existing arrangements under the DM Act 2005 and recommended the merger of the National Calamity Contingency Fund into the National Disaster Response Fund and the Calamity Relief Fund into the State Disaster Response Fund with effect from April 2010. It further recommended that 75 percent of the SDRF requirement for general category states and 90 percent for special category states should be funded by the Centre. The scope of recognized disasters was widened to include landslides, avalanches, cloudbursts and pest attacks in addition to the previously covered cyclone, drought, earthquake, fire, flood and hailstorm, while chemical, industrial and transport accidents would continue to be handled by the respective ministries. Claims on disaster relief funds are made by state governments through memoranda assessed by central teams, reviewed by an inter ministerial group and finally recommended to a High Level Committee consisting of senior ministers and the Deputy Chairman of the Planning Commission for approval. These funds support immediate relief and rehabilitation but not reconstruction, for which Additional Central Assistance has been provided in past disasters like the Gujarat earthquake of 2001, the 2004 tsunami, the 2005 Kashmir earthquake and the 2008 Kosi floods. In 2008–09 alone Rs 645 crore was released under ACA to ten states for long term reconstruction. NDMA has been tasked with setting minimum standards of relief covering relief camps, support to widows and orphans, ex gratia assistance for loss of life and housing damage and restoration of livelihoods, with State Executive Committees holding executive powers to ensure compliance. Sections 46 and 47 of the DM Act 2005 establish the NDRF for emergency response, relief and rehabilitation and the National Disaster Mitigation Fund for projects focused on mitigation, with a revolving fund of Rs 250 crore given to the NDRF for pooled procurement of relief material. Similar funds are to be created at state and district levels, and disaster mitigation should be integrated into development plans as recommended by the Second Administrative Reforms Commission and the Eleventh Five Year Plan. The Commission emphasized that state planning should prioritize hazard identification and risk assessment, though in the absence of reliable data an expenditure based approach continues to be used. The Public Liability Insurance Act of 1991 and the Environmental Relief Fund established in 2008 with a corpus of Rs 285 crore provide additional resources for man made disasters. Effective disaster response further requires trained manpower, continuous capacity building and awareness creation, and an additional grant of Rs 525 crore was recommended to strengthen administrative machinery at state level for disaster management planning and training.

metadata.agency: Government of India, NDMA, UGC, DST, MHRD, AICTE

chunk: Landslide Education emphasizes that landslide education must address preparedness, mitigation, and response efforts by using case histories of past events as inputs. It highlights the need for developing high quality education materials, textbooks, field training, and high standards of teaching at all levels, with training programmes focused on enhancing the capacity and skills of trainers, teachers, NGOs, volunteers, and professionals engaged in disaster management. Science and technology courses must be designed and introduced to orient diverse target groups in landslide management. Education of Professionals stresses that universities and knowledge institutions should revise syllabi and expand teaching of earth sciences, civil engineering, geotechnical engineering, seismology, and structural engineering with practical bias. It calls for new educational programmes and training of engineers and architects to ensure classroom knowledge benefits professional practice, and for retraining of professionals to improve the quality of teaching, textbooks, and training kits in landslide management. Professionals must be trained in correct diagnosis of slope instability, damage and loss assessment, and systematic slope investigation. Geomorphologists must link macro and micro geomorphology with slope analysis, engineering geologists must study micro geological details, and geotechnical engineers should adopt modern soil mechanics tools to characterize landslides. The education should also emphasize that ad hoc measures without scientific investigation are costly and ineffective. Technical institutes and universities in vulnerable areas must develop expertise and state governments, with support from UGC, DST, MHRD and AICTE, should introduce short term Quality Improvement Programmes for teachers and professionals. New technical programmes on landslide management should be launched for college teachers, geoscientists, civil engineers and town planners, bridging the gap between demand and availability of quality teachers skilled in landslide assessment and mitigation. Disaster medicine covering trauma care, epidemic control, paramedics, emergency technicians, and telemedicine should be integrated into undergraduate education to prepare doctors for emergencies. Architecture and civil engineering graduates must be trained in landslide hazards in hilly areas. These efforts require regular curriculum updates, facility upgrades, and institutional capacity building mechanisms. Landslide management should be mainstreamed in development planning through strengthened infrastructure in technical institutions, modernized labs and libraries, and pilot research projects on landslides using advanced technology. Results from these projects should feed into updated technical documents to support sensitisation and training programmes, thereby creating confidence among geoscientists, engineers, and communities in managing landslides effectively.

metadata.agency: Government of India, GSI, DGMs, NDMA

chunk: Community Education and Awareness emphasizes that community education is critical since communities are usually the first responders to disasters and play a prime role in reducing damage. The government and communities must work together to evolve joint action plans for spreading awareness and developing community leadership. Such awareness efforts help ensure safer constructions and empower vulnerable groups including unemployed youth, physically challenged individuals, elderly, women, and school children. Training highlights the importance of involving geologists from the Geological Survey of India and State Directorates of Geology and Mining as well as engineers and professionals working on civil and infrastructural projects in landslide-prone regions. These groups should receive systematic training through in-class and field modules. Training of professionals should focus on exposing them to the latest global developments in landslide investigations and management so that a skilled workforce familiar with modern technological advances is available. Professionals should be sent abroad periodically to acquire theoretical knowledge and practical experience, and training programmes must be interdisciplinary, pilot tested, peer reviewed, and updated regularly. Initial training should be imparted to engineers, geologists, geophysicists, and hydrologists in developmental departments and local bodies of hill regions, ensuring that design directorates employ architects and engineers skilled in landslide-safe design. A training-of-trainers model should be developed so trained professionals disseminate knowledge through professional societies, and minimum safety standards such as BIS codes should be integrated with licensing. The recommended training areas include geomorphological, geotechnical, hydro-geological and GIS-based landslide hazard mapping, geotechnical investigations with slope characterization and stability analysis, slope monitoring and correlation with rainfall and piezometric data, slope modeling, GIS-based hazard, vulnerability, and risk assessments, earthquake-induced landslide analysis in microzonation, slope and avalanche instrumentation, early warning systems, design of landslide control technologies, training of first responders in search, rescue, and medicare, training of communities and local bodies, and training for media professionals to improve disaster reporting. Capacity Development calls for identifying active institutions in the landslide field and strengthening them to improve expertise, resources, and effectiveness in hazard management. The goal is to create a nationwide organized, proactive, systematic, and scientific institutional mechanism to replace the current piecemeal and ad hoc practices. This involves enhancing the expertise of knowledge centers across the country to provide dependable geomorphological, geotechnical, and hydro-geological investigations and ensuring scientific design and timely implementation of mitigation measures. Select institutions in all states and union territories should be strengthened, with expanded mandates to provide both routine and specialized pre- and post-landslide functions.

metadata.agency: Geological Survey of India, NIDM, IIRS, NRSA, SDMAs

chunk: Awareness highlights that as part of landslide hazard mitigation efforts, the Geological Survey of India in consultation with the Ministry of Home Affairs initiated programmes with state governments in landslide affected regions to create awareness among officials and agencies dealing with natural hazards through audio-visual presentations, booklets, and posters explaining causes, terminologies, and treatment measures. The GSI Training Institute conducts regular training on landslide investigations while the National Institute of Disaster Management develops training modules, research, documentation, educational materials, and promotes awareness for hazard mitigation and preparedness. Institutions like the Indian Institute of Remote Sensing, Dehradun, and the Decision Support Centre at NRSA conduct short and long-term courses for using remote sensing and earth observation data for geological hazard management. Mass awareness generation is to become an essential component of disaster mitigation plans implemented through electronic and print media, interactive meets, and distribution of handbills and posters in local languages, with NGOs, state authorities, and volunteers contributing after proper orientation. Audio-visual resources are to be prepared and widely distributed for this purpose. The lack of expertise among many engineers and geologists in hazard management is noted, and the need for including landslide management, risk assessment, and remediation practices in technical education curricula is emphasized. Local communities aware of hazards are better prepared to face consequences, but awareness has historically been low compared to other disasters because landslides are localized, sudden, and often forgotten quickly, despite cumulative losses in hilly terrain being higher than those from other hazards. State governments and State Disaster Management Authorities in collaboration with GSI and other stakeholders are tasked with mobilizing communities for mitigation, supported by awareness campaigns through electronic and print media. These campaigns will target residents in vulnerable areas, highlight risks and vulnerabilities, and clearly communicate roles and responsibilities of communities, institutions, and stakeholders in mitigating landslide impacts.

metadata.agency: Geological Survey of India, State Disaster Management Authorities, Ministry of Home Affairs, NIDM

chunk: Creation of Public Awareness on Landslide Risk Reduction emphasizes that handbooks, posters and handbills containing information on landslide hazards, indicators, precautions and suggestive measures should be distributed and displayed near landslide prone sites, translated into local languages, and supported by short video films and campaigns integrated with print and electronic media. Communities need to be alerted about disaster threats, projected landslide scenarios, lessons from past disasters, early indicators, exposed infrastructure, and the responsibilities of government, local bodies, and the community itself. Awareness drives for specific target groups are essential, with particular focus on sensitizing stakeholders such as construction companies and contractors engaged in infrastructure development in hilly regions to incorporate preventive and mitigation measures into their designs. Comprehensive awareness campaigns at national, state, and district levels should disseminate safe practices before, during, and after landslides, highlight vulnerabilities and roles of stakeholders, and use case studies of major landslides for education. Landslide Preparedness calls for systematic development of disaster management plans for landslide prone areas that reflect regional risk profiles and characteristics, with family and community contingency plans, regular mock drills in offices, schools, industries and vulnerable neighborhoods to ensure vigilance and effective emergency response. Exercises should simulate real situations, testing policies, plans, and personnel readiness through orientation seminars, drills, tabletop exercises, functional exercises, and full-scale exercises, covering all possible hazards in a given area. Medical Preparedness stresses that medical disaster management plans must address injuries specific to landslides, including trauma and psychosocial impacts, and ensure awareness across medical teams. Public health facilities should create disaster management plans with surge capacity, conduct regular drills, and identify trauma and psychosocial care teams. High-risk areas should develop mobile hospitals and Quick Reaction Medical Teams for on-site care, while states must maintain updated lists of hospitals, doctors, paramedics, and mental health providers with adequate supplies of medicines and equipment. Animal Care highlights the need for protective measures for both domestic and wild animals, integrating shelter and feeding plans into preparedness strategies led by Departments of Animal Husbandry, recognizing community compassion for animals during disasters and formalizing it into official preparedness and response frameworks.

metadata.agency: Geological Survey of India, State Disaster Management Authorities, Ministry of Home Affairs

chunk: Preparedness Plan for Landslides emphasizes that landslides are serious geologic hazards often triggered by heavy rainfall, rapid snowmelt or earthquakes, capable of devastating impacts without warning. Preparedness involves individual and community awareness of risks, land conditions, and proactive steps to reduce vulnerability. Pre-disaster preparedness measures include developing family and community evacuation plans, learning local emergency response procedures, familiarizing with land around homes to detect signs of slope movement, monitoring storm water drainage patterns and tilting trees, and discussing landslide safety within households. People should understand that landslide insurance is generally unavailable, though in some cases debris flow damage may be covered under flood insurance. Professional geologists and geotechnical experts should be consulted for property ground assessments and corrective measures. Home hazards can be minimized through planting ground cover, constructing retaining walls, building channels or deflection walls in mudflow areas, and ensuring construction is away from steep slopes, ravine mouths and drainage facilities. Warning signs of landslides include sticking doors and windows, cracks in walls and foundations, pulling away of structures, widening ground fissures, breaking utility lines, bulging ground at slope bases, sudden new springs, muddy water in rivers, tilting of fences or trees, and faint rumbling sounds that grow louder as a slide approaches. Evacuation plans should include at least two routes, a family communication plan in case members are separated, an out-of-area contact person, safe meeting points, and utility shut-off knowledge. Emergency supplies should be stocked including food, water, first aid kit, flashlight, radio, medications, cash, important documents, and children’s needs, along with home protection materials like sandbags and plywood. Continuous monitoring of radio or television for official updates is critical. The plan stresses respecting the speed and destructive power of landslides, and integrating insurance where possible, as local insurers may cover mudflows and landslide risks under natural calamities.

metadata.agency: Geological Survey of India, State Disaster Management Authorities, Ministry of Home Affairs

chunk: Landslide Preparedness and Prevention for Oceanside Residents emphasizes that coastal communities are particularly vulnerable to landslides triggered by heavy rainfall and unstable coastal slopes. Authorities are advised to initiate educational programs to inform residents about risk reduction measures. While landslide damage cannot be entirely prevented, property owners bear responsibility for stabilizing their land and reducing risks. Preventive steps include eliminating litter and dry vegetation, monitoring slopes for cracks and holes, and contacting local public works departments for guidance. During a landslide, individuals should move quickly out of the path of debris to stable ground, or if escape is impossible, curl into a tight ball to protect the head and body. Indoors, one should seek cover under sturdy furniture, while outdoors, one should run toward higher ground or shelter near immovable large rocks or trees. After a landslide, residents are urged to avoid slide areas due to risks of secondary slides, gather at designated meeting points, check for injured people without entering dangerous zones, assist vulnerable neighbors, administer first aid if trained, and monitor official broadcasts for updates. Post-landslide safety actions include reporting broken utility lines, inspecting building foundations and chimneys for damage, replanting vegetation to prevent erosion and flash flooding, and consulting experts to design corrective measures without worsening risks. Section 9.8 addresses survival skills, stressing that disaster survival requires preparation, training, and adaptability. Survival training reduces fear, enhances confidence, and builds capacity to live by resourcefulness. Core skills include shelter construction, water sourcing, food foraging, and improvisation without reliance on luxury tools. The survival framework is encapsulated in the acronym SURVIVAL: Size up the situation, Use all senses and avoid haste, Remember your location, Vanquish fear and panic, Improvise, Value living, Act like natives, and Live by your wits while mastering basic skills. These guidelines highlight the importance of community preparedness, individual responsibility, and skill development to enhance resilience against coastal landslide disasters.

Search and Rescue (SAR) operations in a landslide debris field must commence with a thorough size-up and risk assessment. Teams must identify and continuously monitor the headscarp and flanks of the slide for signs of secondary movement. A dedicated 'slope watcher' with an air horn must be posted at all times to provide an immediate evacuation signal for the rescue teams.

Initial search actions should be non-invasive to avoid causing further collapse of potential voids. Teams should perform a systematic 'call and listen' or hailing search across the entire debris surface, followed by the deployment of canine SAR teams and acoustic sensors to detect signs of life before any heavy digging begins.

A grid search pattern must be established over the debris field, with each grid square systematically searched. Markers, such as flags on poles, must be used to indicate which areas have been cleared by hasty search, canine teams, and technical search, ensuring no areas are missed.

Probing for victims should be done systematically using collapsible avalanche probes or rebar. Probes should be inserted vertically at regular intervals. Rescuers must learn to differentiate the feel of hitting a body versus debris like wood or rock. If a potential victim is located, the probe is left in place as a marker for the excavation team.

Excavation must be done with extreme care. Hand tools should be used to carefully remove debris from around a located victim. The goal is to 'uncover' the victim, not to 'dig them out,' to prevent further injury. Shoring and cribbing materials must be on hand to stabilize the trench or pit as it is dug to protect both victim and rescuers from collapse.

Information from survivors is a critical search asset. Conduct interviews to determine the likely locations of missing persons within a buried structure (e.g., bedrooms if the slide was at night, living areas if during the day). This intelligence allows for targeted searching rather than random digging.

Heavy machinery, such as excavators, should only be used under the direct guidance of a technical search specialist. They are used for carefully removing large layers of overburden from areas already confirmed clear by search teams, or for moving large, hazardous objects. They must never be used for speculative digging.

The presence of water flowing through the debris field poses a significant hazard. It can undermine the stability of the debris pile and create 'quicksand' like conditions. All SAR personnel must wear a PFD (Personal Flotation Device) if working in or around water-saturated debris.

Technical search teams should use search cameras (borescopes) to inspect voids and confined spaces before entry. These cameras can help locate victims, assess the stability of the space, and identify potential atmospheric hazards without risking a rescuer.

SAR operations should transition to recovery mode only after all viable search methods have been exhausted and the window of survivability has passed. This decision must be made by the Incident Commander based on expert medical and technical advice, and communicated sensitively to the families of the missing.

A robust personnel accountability system is mandatory. Every rescuer entering the debris field must have a tag on a command board. Team leaders must conduct regular check-ins (PARs) every 15-20 minutes to confirm the safety and status of all members.

Recognize clues that indicate the location of a buried structure, such as utility lines leading into the ground, remnants of a foundation, or a cluster of personal belongings. The debris flow pattern can also provide clues about where structures were moved.

A clear command structure must be established with designated sectors (e.g., Alpha, Bravo) within the slide area. Each sector has a supervisor who directs search efforts in their zone and reports directly to the SAR Operations Chief, ensuring manageable spans of control.

Night operations in a landslide zone are exceptionally dangerous due to reduced visibility of hazards. They should only be undertaken for a confirmed, viable victim. The site must be extensively illuminated, and the number of personnel on the pile should be kept to an absolute minimum.

All rescuers must undergo a formal decontamination and debriefing after each operational period. Decontamination removes physical contaminants, while the debriefing (Critical Incident Stress Debriefing) addresses the significant psychological trauma associated with landslide rescue and recovery.

Medical triage must prioritize victims with signs of airway obstruction or severe respiratory distress from dust inhalation or burial (asphyxiation), and those with active, severe bleeding. These are the most immediate life threats. These patients are categorized as Red (Immediate).

A high index of suspicion for crush syndrome must be maintained for any victim who has been trapped or compressed by debris for more than 30 minutes. Before extricating the victim, advanced medical personnel should, if possible, administer IV fluids to mitigate the release of toxins (rhabdomyolysis) that can cause kidney failure upon pressure release.

Prioritize transport for Red-tagged patients with survivable injuries. Yellow-tagged patients, such as those with isolated limb fractures but stable vital signs, can wait for transport. Green-tagged patients ('walking wounded') with minor cuts and bruises should be directed to a separate area for first aid to declutter the critical triage zone.

Triage should occur at a designated Casualty Collection Point (CCP) located on safe, stable ground, away from but with accessible entry from the immediate slide area. This ensures a safe environment for medical personnel to work and prevents them from being endangered by secondary slides.

Assume spinal injury for any victim of a landslide who has been thrown, tumbled, or caught in moving debris. Full spinal immobilization with a cervical collar and backboard must be applied before moving the patient, unless they are in immediate danger of being re-buried.

Hypothermia is a major, often overlooked threat, even in temperate climates, as victims can be buried in cold, wet mud. All extricated patients must be immediately removed from wet clothing, dried, and wrapped in blankets or thermal coverings to prevent heat loss, which can worsen shock and trauma.

Prioritization of search areas should be based on a combination of survivor information, structural damage assessment, and canine alerts. A confirmed 'live hit' from a search dog in one area takes immediate priority over speculative digging in another, regardless of the number of people believed to be missing there.

Resource allocation must be prioritized by the Incident Commander. Life-saving equipment, such as specialized rescue teams and medical helicopters, is to be assigned to the highest-priority tasks (e.g., extricating a known survivor) rather than logistical tasks like moving supplies, until all rescue opportunities are exhausted.

A dedicated psychological first aid station should be established near the triage area. Its purpose is to triage and manage survivors experiencing acute psychological trauma (the 'psychologically stunned'). This prevents them from wandering into dangerous areas or overwhelming medical staff needed for physically injured patients.

Victims tagged as Deceased (Black) should be left in situ, if doing so does not hinder the rescue of live victims. Their location must be marked and recorded. Recovery of the deceased is a secondary priority after all live rescue operations have ceased.

Prioritize the re-opening of a single, stable access route to the disaster site. This 'lifeline' route is critical for moving patients out and bringing in heavy rescue equipment and personnel. All debris-clearing efforts should be focused on this one route until it is fully operational.

The Triage Officer must keep a running tally of patients by category (e.g., Red: 5, Yellow: 12, Green: 30). This information is continuously relayed to the Medical Communications Officer, who uses it to request the correct number and type of ambulances and to alert receiving hospitals of the incoming patient load.

Special attention must be given to the triage of rescuers themselves. A dedicated medical station for responders should be set up to treat injuries, exhaustion, and dehydration, ensuring the rescue workforce remains effective and safe.

In large-scale events where resources are overwhelmed, triage may need to shift to a 'reverse triage' model for evacuation, where the Green-tagged 'walking wounded' are evacuated first under their own power to free up transport and personnel for more critical patients.

Do not remove triage tags after a patient has been treated. The initial tag color provides critical information to hospital staff about the patient's condition at the scene. A secondary tag can be added to reflect their current status, but the original must remain.

Pre-evacuation planning must identify individuals with significant mobility impairments who live in high-risk zones. A dedicated 'evacuation assistance team' of trained volunteers or emergency responders must be assigned to these specific individuals to ensure they can be moved safely and quickly, as they cannot self-evacuate.

Transport for special-needs individuals may not be possible with vehicles. Teams must be equipped with and trained to use alternative transport devices like the 'stokes basket' or specialized evacuation chairs that can be carried by multiple people over rough, uneven landslide debris.

When evacuating individuals dependent on electrical medical equipment (e.g., oxygen concentrators, ventilators), teams must carry portable power packs or battery backups. The evacuation plan must account for the limited runtime of these devices and prioritize the swiftest possible transport to a location with stable power.

Evacuation shelters must have a designated, pre-wired area for special-needs evacuees that provides reliable, generator-backed power outlets for life-sustaining medical equipment. This area should be on the ground floor and have accessible restroom facilities.

A 'go-kit' for special-needs evacuees should be prepared in advance. Responders should assist them in gathering this kit, which must include a 7-day supply of all medications, copies of prescriptions, medical records, and contact information for their doctors.

During a rapid evacuation, children, particularly toddlers and infants, are a special-needs group. Ensure each child has identification on their person. Rescue teams should carry emergency infant formula and diapers, as parents may have had to flee without them.

Evacuation alerts must be accessible. For the hearing impaired, this includes high-intensity strobe lights on emergency vehicles and text-based alerts. For the visually impaired, this includes loud, clear verbal instructions through sirens and public address systems.

Nursing homes and care facilities in landslide risk zones require specialized evacuation plans. These plans must involve agreements with receiving facilities outside the risk zone and contracts with accessible transportation providers, activated at the earliest warning stage.

Responders must be trained to communicate with individuals with cognitive disabilities or dementia. Use simple, direct sentences; maintain eye contact; and provide reassurance. If possible, ensure a known caregiver is evacuated with them to minimize distress and confusion.

Service animals are essential medical equipment for their handlers and must be evacuated with them. Rescue vehicles and shelters must accommodate these animals. Plans should include a small stockpile of basic animal food and water bowls.

At the shelter, the intake process for special-needs individuals must include a detailed assessment by a registered nurse or medic to identify their specific health, dietary, and accessibility needs, which are then communicated to the shelter management team.

For communities with non-native speakers, evacuation teams should be equipped with translation cards or apps that have pre-loaded emergency phrases. The 'point-at-the-picture' style communication board is highly effective for conveying urgent instructions across language barriers.

Populations requiring regular, life-sustaining treatment like dialysis or chemotherapy must be evacuated pre-emptively at the earliest warning stage. The plan must include their immediate transport to a functioning medical facility that can continue their treatment without interruption.

In the aftermath of the 2017 Regent landslide, a disproportionate number of casualties were individuals with mobility issues who were unable to flee the fast-moving debris flow. This underscores the critical need for proactive, targeted evacuation assistance plans.

A 'buddy system' should be established in high-risk communities, where able-bodied volunteers are paired with elderly or disabled neighbors. The volunteer's role is to ensure their buddy receives the evacuation warning and to provide immediate assistance or alert emergency services on their behalf.

Family reunification efforts must anticipate a high number of fatalities. Desks should be co-located with grief counseling and mental health support services, as inquiries may quickly transition into death notifications. Staff must be trained in delivering difficult news with compassion.

For any located victims, whether alive or deceased, their exact location of discovery (with GPS coordinates if possible) must be meticulously documented. This data is vital for helping forensic teams identify remains and for providing answers to families about their loved ones' final moments.

The inquiry form for missing persons in a landslide must include a section for the last known location of the person's home and the specific room they were likely in. This information directly assists SAR teams in targeting their search efforts within the massive debris field.

A central repository for photographs of the missing should be established. Families provide photos, which are then used by SAR teams, mortuary services, and hospital staff to aid in visual identification of victims, both survivors who are incapacitated and the deceased.

Because entire families may be missing, reunification efforts must extend to the next of kin and the wider community. Public lists of survivors should be posted at shelters and community hubs to allow neighbors and friends to identify who is safe and report who is still unaccounted for.

All personal effects found on or near a victim must be carefully bagged, labeled with the location found, and turned over to the authorities. These items are often crucial for identification and are of immense sentimental value to the family.

The process for reuniting an unaccompanied child must be exceptionally rigorous. If the parents are missing or deceased, the child must be placed into the care of pre-vetted child protection services, not handed over to individuals claiming to be relatives without a thorough verification process.

Public messaging should clearly direct families to a single, centralized phone number and physical location for all reunification inquiries. This prevents families from having to contact multiple hospitals, shelters, and police stations, which adds to their distress.

A family liaison officer should be assigned to each family with missing members. This officer provides regular, coordinated updates from SAR, medical, and coroner's offices, ensuring the family receives accurate information from a single, trusted source.

For unidentified deceased victims, Disaster Victim Identification (DVI) protocols must be activated. This involves collecting postmortem data (dental records, fingerprints, DNA) and comparing it with antemortem data provided by families to achieve scientific identification.

Reunification databases need a specific field for 'presumed deceased based on location.' This is used when a house is confirmed to have been completely destroyed and is inaccessible, helping to manage family expectations while search efforts continue.

Reunification is not just about the living. The process must also include arranging for families to identify and claim the bodies of their loved ones in a respectful and culturally appropriate manner, with support from spiritual care providers.

After a successful reunification, a follow-up welfare check should be conducted within 24-48 hours. This ensures the reunited family has access to shelter, food, water, and mental health support, as the initial relief can quickly give way to the overwhelming reality of their loss.

In the Oso, Washington landslide, the lack of a centralized missing persons list in the initial hours hampered the search. Community-led efforts to build a list on social media and whiteboards proved vital, highlighting the need for official systems to be rapidly deployable and accessible.

Provide a secure, private area for families to make inquiries. Discussing the details of missing loved ones is deeply personal and traumatic, and should not be done in a public, open-plan registration hall.

The evacuation center registration form must include a mandatory field for the evacuee's full residential address, including any informal local names for the area. This data is immediately relayed to the SAR command post to cross-reference with maps of the slide zone, helping to identify which households are safe and which are likely impacted.

Registration staff must be trained to ask, 'Is there anyone else who was at your home who is not with you now?' This proactive question is more effective at identifying missing persons than simply asking if the family is okay, as survivors may be too disoriented to volunteer the information.

A large, physical map of the affected area should be placed at the registration center. Survivors can point to their home's location, which is often more accurate than a written address in rural or informal settlements. Each marked house is then flagged as 'Accounted For' to help narrow the search area.

The registration database must be shared in real-time between all official shelters, hospitals, and the SAR command post. As a survivor registers at any location, their status as 'Safe' is immediately visible to all other agencies, preventing duplication of search efforts for someone who has already been found.

When registering a family, it is crucial to record the names and ages of all members, not just a total number. This detailed list is essential for cross-referencing with school rosters, workplace records, and missing persons reports to ensure every individual is accounted for.

Registration teams should include mental health professionals. The process of recounting who is missing and where they were can be extremely traumatic for survivors. On-the-spot psychological first aid can provide immediate support and build trust.

In addition to individual registration, a 'community leader check-in' should be established. Local leaders, elders, or religious figures often have deep knowledge of who lives where and can quickly help identify entire families that are unaccounted for, providing a rapid assessment of the potential scale of loss.

Registrars should photograph each person or head of household upon registration (with consent). This visual record, linked to their registration data, is invaluable for resolving identification issues later, especially if evacuees lose their ID cards.

The registration process must capture immediate needs. A simple checklist on the form for 'Food', 'Water', 'Medical', 'Shelter', 'Mental Health Support' allows for a rapid triage of needs for the entire shelter population, guiding the allocation of aid.

Data privacy is paramount. Registration information, especially lists of the missing or deceased, must be treated as confidential. Publicly posting lists of fatalities is prohibited; this information must be delivered directly to the next of kin by trained personnel.

For evacuees arriving from areas completely cut off, the registration process is also an intelligence-gathering opportunity. Ask about the status of roads, bridges, and other landmarks to help build a more complete operational picture of the disaster zone.

Mobile registration teams should be deployed to informal gathering points where survivors may have congregated but have not yet made it to an official center. This proactive approach ensures that even the most isolated survivors are accounted for.

The registration kit should include wristbands with unique serial numbers. The wristband number is recorded on the paper form and entered into the database, creating a reliable link between the individual and their record for aid distribution and tracking.

Analysis from past landslide events indicates that the first 12 hours of registration are the most critical for SAR. The accuracy and speed of collecting information on who is safe directly correlates with the ability of rescue teams to focus their efforts on credible, targeted locations, saving lives.

At check-out, the registration system must capture the evacuee's intended destination. This helps track secondary displacement and allows authorities to provide information about aid available in those new areas.

Hazard Action: If caught in a landslide, immediately move away from its path. Run sideways, perpendicular to the flow, not downhill. Getting out of the direct channel is the single most important action. If escape is impossible, curl into a tight ball and protect your head with your arms and hands.

Hazard Action: In a multi-story building during a landslide warning, move to the highest floor on the side of the building furthest away from the approaching slide. Do not shelter in lower floors as they are most likely to be crushed or buried.

Hazard Action: After a landslide, stay away from the slide area. The ground remains unstable and secondary slides or debris flows are very common, often triggered by rainfall or even the vibrations from rescue equipment. Do not return to inspect your property until experts declare it safe.

Hazard Action: Be aware of and report any tilted or broken utility lines. Landslides can rupture gas, water, and sewer lines, creating fire, health, and electrocution hazards. Report any hissing sounds or the smell of gas immediately from a safe distance.

Hazard Action: Landslide debris can create dams in rivers and streams. These 'landslide dams' can fail suddenly, causing a catastrophic downstream flood. If you are downstream from a slide, be alert for sudden changes in water level and be prepared to move to high ground instantly.

Hazard Action: If you see someone trapped in a shallow slide, do not rush in to help without assessing the stability of the ground around them. Your own weight could trigger a further slip. If possible, try to reach them with a rope or a long object from stable ground while calling for professional help.

Hazard Action: Long-term risk reduction involves landscape management. Do not build homes at the top or bottom of steep slopes. Implement proper drainage systems to direct water away from hillsides and foundations. Plant ground cover and native vegetation, as their roots help hold soil in place.

Hazard Action: Pay attention to the physical signs around your property that indicate slope instability. These include saturated ground in previously dry areas, new seeps or springs appearing on slopes, and small slips or slumps of earth. Report these to local authorities as they are early warning signs.

Hazard Action: If you must cross a debris field on foot after the event, be aware that it can contain sharp objects like broken glass, metal, and wood. The ground can also have hidden voids or pockets of soft mud. Use a probing stick or pole to test the ground in front of you before you step.

Hazard Action: If your vehicle is caught in a debris flow, stay inside the vehicle and keep your seatbelt on. The vehicle can provide a cocoon of protection from initial impact trauma. Once movement stops, assess the situation before attempting to exit.

Hazard Action: After a landslide, water sources like wells and springs may be contaminated. Do not drink the water without boiling or purifying it first. Wait for health authorities to test and clear water sources for public use.

Hazard Action: When re-entering a damaged home, look up before you look down. Check the stability of the ceiling and roof structure for any new cracks or sagging. Debris from above can fall unexpectedly during aftershocks or as the ground settles.

Hazard Action: Have a family communication plan. Designate an out-of-town contact person that all family members can call or text to report they are safe. This is often more reliable than trying to call each other directly when local phone networks are overloaded or down.

Hazard Action: Consult a geotechnical expert before rebuilding on a property that has been affected by a landslide. The underlying stability of the ground may be permanently compromised, and mitigation measures or relocation may be necessary to ensure future safety.

Hazard Action: Understand that even small, human-caused changes can trigger landslides. Cutting into the base of a slope to create a flat yard or road, or piling soil and waste onto a slope, can remove support and add weight, leading to failure during heavy rains. Avoid such modifications without professional engineering advice.

A 'Code Black' hospital status is declared when all road access is severed by landslides, completely isolating the facility. This is a critical red flag indicating the hospital is now a 'resource island' and must function entirely on its own, triggering extreme conservation of all supplies and immediate notification to regional command of its inability to receive patients or send them out.

Any visible structural damage to the hospital caused by ground shifting, such as large cracks in the foundation, sheared utility lines, or distorted building frames, constitutes a red flag. The affected wing or building must be immediately evacuated, and a structural engineer's assessment is required before re-entry.

A hospital is flagged as 'Red' when its capacity to manage severe trauma (orthopedic, neurosurgical) is completely overwhelmed, with all operating theaters occupied and a backlog of more than five critical (T1) patients waiting for surgery. At this point, it must request air evacuation for new critical patients to other centers.

The rupture of a hospital's main oxygen supply line due to ground movement is a critical red flag. This necessitates an immediate manual switch to backup cylinders for all ventilated patients and an emergency inventory to determine operational endurance, which could be mere hours.

A red flag is raised when the morgue capacity is exceeded. This presents a biohazard and a significant psychological stressor. The hospital must activate its mass fatality plan, which includes designating and preparing a secure, refrigerated temporary morgue area.

Contamination of the hospital's water source by mud or debris is a red flag. All water must be considered non-potable, and the facility must switch to bottled water for drinking and sterile water for all clinical procedures until the system is flushed and tested.

A hospital must raise a red flag when its blood bank inventory drops below the minimum required to manage one major trauma surgery (e.g., less than 10 units of O-negative blood) and resupply is impossible due to isolation. This triggers a 'walking blood bank' protocol among staff and the community if safe.

The inability to communicate with regional emergency command for more than two hours due to damaged infrastructure is a red flag. The hospital must assume it is on its own and attempt to establish contact via satellite phone, radio, or by dispatching a runner if any route is passable.

If a new, unstable slope or headscarp is identified above the hospital, posing a direct threat of a secondary landslide hitting the facility, this is the most severe red flag. It requires immediate, emergency evacuation of all patients and staff to a pre-designated safe location, even if it is an open field.

A hospital is flagged when the influx of psychologically traumatized but physically uninjured people (the 'worried well') begins to impede the treatment of critically injured patients. Security must be posted at the emergency entrance to gently but firmly redirect non-urgent cases to a designated first-aid post.

For severe bleeding from a limb, the primary first-aid procedure is to apply a tourniquet. Place it 2-3 inches above the wound, not on a joint. Tighten until the bleeding stops. Write the time of application on the tourniquet or the patient's forehead. This is a life-saving measure for arterial bleeding common in trauma.

For crush injuries where a victim is pinned, do not attempt to remove the heavy object. The first-aid goal is to provide supportive care: keep them warm, provide fluids if they are conscious, and await a specialized USAR (Urban Search and Rescue) team. Rapid release can cause deadly crush syndrome.

For open fractures, where bone is visible, do not try to push the bone back in. Cover the wound with a clean, damp cloth. Splint the limb as it lies, immobilizing the joints above and below the fracture to prevent further damage during transport.

Inhalation of dust and fine particles is common. For patients with coughing and difficulty breathing, the first-aid step is to move them to an area with cleaner air if possible. Sit them in a comfortable position, leaning slightly forward, to ease breathing. Provide water to soothe their throat.

For head injuries with altered consciousness, the priority is maintaining an open airway using the jaw-thrust maneuver (not the head-tilt-chin-lift) to protect the spine. The patient should be placed in the recovery position if breathing on their own, and kept warm to prevent shock.

First-aid stations must be prepared for a high volume of minor wounds (cuts, abrasions). The procedure is a 'high-pressure wash' with clean water to dislodge dirt and bacteria, followed by application of an antiseptic and dressing. This aggressive cleaning is vital to prevent wound infections.

To make an effective makeshift splint, it must be rigid, long enough to immobilize the joint above and below the injury, and well-padded. Items like trekking poles, firm tree branches, or rolled-up mats can be used. Secure it with bandages, belts, or strips of cloth.

For a suspected pelvic fracture (indicated by severe pain and instability), bind the pelvis tightly with a sheet or belt wrapped around the level of the hip bones. This is a temporary measure to reduce internal bleeding until proper medical care is available.

Every first-aid provider must wear gloves, and if possible, eye protection. The risk of exposure to bloodborne pathogens is extremely high when managing the severe traumatic injuries typical of a landslide.

For abdominal evisceration, where organs are exposed, do not try to push them back in. Cover the wound and organs with a clean, moist dressing (using sterile water or saline). Cover this with a dry dressing to keep the patient warm and prevent heat loss.

Spinal injury is the default assumption for any person buried, thrown, or found unconscious after a landslide. All responders must approach with this in mind and apply manual in-line stabilization of the head and neck as the very first step of patient contact.

During extrication from debris, spinal integrity must be maintained at all costs. This may require digging around the patient to create enough space for a long spine board, rather than pulling the patient out. The rescuer holding the head (C-spine) is in charge of all patient movement commands.

Use of a Kendrick Extrication Device (KED) or a similar vest-style immobilizer is preferred for removing patients from a seated position or confined space, as it provides more comprehensive support to the thoracic and lumbar spine than a collar alone.

A key sign of neurogenic shock from a high spinal cord injury is warm, dry skin below the level of injury, accompanied by low blood pressure and a slow heart rate. This is a medical emergency requiring aggressive fluid resuscitation and vasopressors, and immediate evacuation.

When moving a spinally immobilized patient across highly unstable or steep terrain, a 'belay' or rope safety line should be attached to the spine board or stokes basket to prevent dropping the patient in case of a slip. This requires coordination between the rescue and rope-technician teams.

Clearing a patient's spine in the field is not recommended. All patients immobilized for a suspected spinal injury should remain immobilized until they can be fully evaluated with imaging at a hospital, even if they regain consciousness and claim to feel fine.

Asking the conscious patient about their symptoms is vital. Questions like 'Can you feel me touching your fingers/toes?' and 'Can you wiggle your fingers/toes?' provide a quick neurological baseline. Document the findings and re-check frequently.

If a rigid cervical collar is not available, a makeshift one can be fashioned from a densely rolled towel, wrapped around the neck and secured with tape. While not ideal, it serves as a reminder to the patient and rescuers to minimize movement.

The use of a vacuum splint or vacuum mattress is highly effective for spinal immobilization, as it conforms to the patient's body, providing better support and comfort than a hard board, which is particularly important for long or difficult transports.

Any patient with a significant head injury should also be treated as having a spinal injury until proven otherwise. The forces required to cause a serious head injury are often sufficient to damage the cervical spine.

Seizures in a landslide context are often post-traumatic, resulting from a head injury. During the seizure, protect the person's head and spine as much as possible, as uncontrolled movements can worsen existing injuries. Do not restrain their limbs.

After a seizure, especially if a head injury is suspected, the patient must be assessed for signs of increased intracranial pressure, such as a worsening headache, vomiting, unequal pupils, or decreasing level of consciousness. This is a critical emergency.

In a field or shelter setting, a person with a known seizure disorder who has been evacuated without their medication is a high-risk patient. They should be prioritized for transfer to a medical facility to get their anti-epileptic drug regimen restarted to prevent breakthrough seizures.

The occurrence of a first-time seizure in any landslide survivor is a medical emergency. It indicates a probable traumatic brain injury, and the patient requires urgent helicopter evacuation to a trauma center with neurosurgical capabilities.

When managing a seizure in a debris field, the rescuer's priority is to protect the patient from further harm from sharp objects or unstable surfaces. If possible, clear a small area around the person. Do not attempt to move them until the seizure has finished unless their location is imminently dangerous.

Post-seizure (postictal) confusion can be profound and last for a long time. In a chaotic rescue environment, it is vital to stay with the person, calmly reorienting them and ensuring they do not wander into a hazardous area.

Field medical packs for landslide response should include benzodiazepines (e.g., injectable or intranasal midazolam) for the management of status epilepticus (a seizure lasting more than 5 minutes), to be administered by trained paramedics or medical personnel.

All information about a seizure event (time of onset, duration, type of movements, post-seizure behavior) must be documented and relayed to the next level of care. This information is diagnostically critical for the receiving hospital.
Placing the person in the recovery position after the seizure is vital for airway management, especially in a dusty environment, as it helps prevent aspiration of dirt, saliva, or vomit.
Do not give a person anything to eat or drink until they are fully awake and alert after a seizure, as their ability to swallow may be impaired, posing a choking risk.

Any pregnant woman who has sustained trauma, however minor, requires immediate medical evaluation. The priority is to stabilize the mother, as her well-being is directly linked to the fetus. Address airway, breathing, and circulation (ABC) for the mother first.

The stress and physical trauma of a landslide can induce preterm labor or cause placental abruption. Any pregnant evacuee reporting contractions, abdominal pain, or vaginal bleeding must be triaged as high priority and evacuated to a facility with surgical and neonatal capabilities.

If an emergency delivery must happen in a field setting after a landslide, finding the cleanest possible surface is the top priority. Use a clean plastic sheet if available. Ensure the person delivering the baby has washed their hands thoroughly and is wearing sterile gloves.

When evacuating a pregnant woman over difficult, uneven terrain, special care must be taken to avoid falls and jostling. If she is on a backboard, ensure she is well-padded and secured. If she is beyond 20 weeks gestation, tilt the board 15-30 degrees to the left to displace the uterus off the major blood vessels.
Shelters must prioritize pregnant and lactating women for supplemental nutrition, clean drinking water, and hygienic sanitation facilities to protect their health and the health of their infant.

For a pregnant patient in cardiac arrest, CPR should be performed with one modification: if she is visibly pregnant, a rescuer should manually displace the uterus to the left side to improve blood flow back to the heart. Perimortem C-section should be considered by advanced providers if resuscitation is unsuccessful.

During triage, a pregnant woman is considered two patients. Her priority level should be elevated. For example, a pregnant woman with moderate injuries who would normally be Yellow might be upgraded to Red because of the risk to the fetus.
All pregnant women evacuated from a landslide zone should receive a dose of tetanus toxoid if their vaccination status is unknown, due to the high risk of wound contamination.

In the absence of sterile supplies to cut the umbilical cord, the recommendation is to leave the cord intact, keep the baby warm on the mother's abdomen, and keep the placenta level with or slightly above the baby until trained help arrives. This reduces the risk of infection.

The psychological stress of the landslide itself can have severe impacts on a pregnancy. Provide a calm, supportive environment for evacuated pregnant women and ensure they have access to mental health first aid and counseling services.

The top priorities for a newborn delivered in an austere environment are warmth and breathing. Immediately dry the baby, remove the wet cloth, and place the baby skin-to-skin with the mother, covering both with a dry blanket. Stimulate breathing by rubbing the back if necessary.

In a landslide scenario where communities are isolated, a 'buddy system' for new mothers should be created in shelters. Experienced mothers can provide invaluable support and recognize danger signs in the newborn or mother when medical staff are stretched thin.

Airborne dust from a landslide is a major hazard for a neonate's respiratory system. Keep the baby's face covered with a light, breathable cloth when outdoors and shelter them in the least dusty area possible to prevent respiratory distress.

Breastfeeding is a life-saving intervention. The mother's stress from the disaster may temporarily affect milk supply, so it's critical to provide her with extra hydration, nutrition, and reassurance to support successful lactation.

Basic neonatal resuscitation kits, including a bag-valve-mask of the appropriate size and a suction bulb, are essential equipment for any medical team deploying to a landslide-affected area where people are isolated.

Any rescued neonate who is cold to the touch, lethargic, or has a weak cry needs to be actively and slowly rewarmed. Skin-to-skin contact is the safest method. Rapid external rewarming can be dangerous.

Transporting a neonate, especially a premature one, requires special care to maintain warmth. A makeshift incubator can be created by wrapping the baby in layers and placing safe, warm (not hot) water bottles near them, ensuring the bottles do not directly touch the skin.

Ensure every newborn has some form of identification. An ID band with the mother's name and a unique number should be placed on the baby's ankle and wrist to prevent separation or misidentification in a chaotic shelter or hospital environment.

If a mother is killed and a breastfeeding infant survives, finding a wet nurse is a traditional but potentially risky solution due to disease transmission. The priority should be to secure ready-to-use infant formula and sterile feeding equipment, and train a caregiver in its safe preparation.

In the aftermath, it's vital to re-establish routine neonatal care as quickly as possible, including vaccinations. A mobile clinic should be tasked with providing this service to displaced populations to prevent outbreaks of vaccine-preventable diseases.

The primary challenge for chronic care in landslides is access. Mobile medical teams must be deployed by helicopter or on foot to isolated communities to deliver medications and provide clinical assessments for patients cut off from their regular doctors.

For isolated communities, a 'medication hotline' should be established via satellite phone or radio, allowing patients or community leaders to report medication needs to a central command, which can then coordinate airdrops or delivery.

Field teams must be equipped with portable diagnostic tools, like blood pressure cuffs, glucometers, and pulse oximeters, to effectively manage chronic conditions in communities without power or functioning clinics.

When evacuating patients with chronic conditions over difficult terrain, ensure they carry at least a 3-day supply of their essential medications on their person in a waterproof bag. Their main supply might be delayed or lost.

Food aid packages for landslide-affected areas should, where possible, include options suitable for diabetics and hypertensives, such as low-sodium and low-sugar items. If not possible, medical teams must provide intensive education on managing conditions with standard rations.

A simplified chronic disease patient-held record should be distributed. Patients can fill this out with their name, condition, medications, and dosages. This empowers them with their own health information if they are displaced and have to see an unfamiliar doctor.

In isolated villages after the 2015 Nepal earthquake and landslides, community health workers who were trained in basic management of chronic diseases became a critical lifeline, bridging the gap until larger medical teams could arrive.

Patients who have missed doses of beta-blockers for heart conditions should be restarted on a lower dose and slowly titrated up, as sudden resumption of a high dose can cause dangerous side effects. This requires clinical oversight.

Mental health support is a key component of chronic care. The stress of a landslide can severely worsen conditions like hypertension and diabetes. All chronic care consultations should include a check-in on the patient's stress levels and coping mechanisms.

Continuity of care relies on information. Where possible, deploy teams with ruggedized tablets containing electronic health records or medication databases to help identify and correctly manage patients with complex chronic conditions.

For isolated communities, insulin must be packaged for airdrop or transport by foot. It should be placed in a small, hard-sided cooler with cool packs (not frozen) and padding to prevent breakage. The package must be clearly marked as 'MEDICAL - INSULIN - KEEP COOL'.

A key challenge is the lack of refrigeration. Community leaders in isolated villages should be taught evaporative cooling techniques: wrapping the insulin container in a wet cloth and placing it in a shady, breezy area to maintain a temperature below 25°C.

Drones can be a viable option for delivering small, high-value packages like insulin and glucose test strips to individuals in otherwise inaccessible areas. This requires pre-planned flight paths and a recipient on the ground ready to receive the package.

When resupplying an isolated diabetic patient, the package must contain not just insulin but also a corresponding amount of syringes or pen needles, alcohol swabs, and glucose test strips. A glucometer with fresh batteries is also essential. Insulin is useless without the means to administer and monitor it.

In a prolonged isolation scenario, medical teams may need to instruct patients on how to adjust their insulin dosage based on limited food availability and increased physical exertion from walking or labor, to avoid dangerous hypoglycemia.

Any field medical clinic must have a reliable cold chain solution, such as a solar-powered medical refrigerator or a high-performance, long-duration cooler, to serve as a central repository for insulin and other temperature-sensitive medications for the community.

Evacuation teams must ask every patient if they are diabetic and if they have their insulin. If they do, the medical bag is a priority item. If they don't, this information must be immediately relayed to the medical command so a supply can be arranged at the destination.

If insulin's temperature has been compromised, it may still be usable, although its potency may be reduced. Patients should be instructed to use it but monitor their blood glucose much more frequently and watch for signs of hyperglycemia. Discarding it with no replacement is a worse option.

A sharps disposal plan is critical for isolated communities being resupplied with needles. Instruct users to place used sharps in a hard plastic container (like a soda bottle), seal it, label it 'SHARPS', and store it for safe removal once access is restored.

When communicating with isolated diabetic patients, also ask about their supply of hypoglycemia treatments (glucose tabs, juice boxes). These are just as important as insulin and should be included in resupply drops.

The list of registered dialysis patients must be immediately cross-referenced with maps of landslide-affected areas to identify which patients are likely isolated. These individuals become the highest priority for helicopter MEDEVAC, as they cannot survive more than a few missed treatments.

For an isolated dialysis patient who has missed sessions, medical teams must be prepared to manage severe hyperkalemia in the field. This requires a 'hyperkalemia kit' with IV calcium, insulin, dextrose, and nebulized salbutamol, which can be life-saving while awaiting evacuation.

Any dialysis center that has lost power and water or is inaccessible due to landslides must immediately transmit its patient roster to the regional health command to facilitate tracking and outreach to every single patient.

A regional plan must be in place to increase capacity at unaffected dialysis centers, including extending operating hours to 24/7 and bringing in extra staff to handle the influx of evacuated patients from the disaster zone.

Instruct isolated patients to immediately begin their emergency renal diet and fluid restrictions. Radio broadcasts should include public service announcements detailing these life-saving dietary measures for any kidney patients who may be listening.

When found, a dialysis patient who has missed treatments may be confused or weak due to electrolyte imbalances. They should be treated as a critical medical patient, handled gently, and provided with immediate medical assessment before transport.

Patients on peritoneal dialysis (PD) are at extreme risk if their sterile supplies are destroyed. Resupply of PD fluid is a high priority. They also need a dedicated, clean space to perform exchanges to prevent peritonitis, which is fatal without prompt treatment.

Potassium-binding agents, such as sodium polystyrene sulfonate, should be included in medical packs being sent to isolated areas. These can be administered orally to help temporarily lower potassium levels in patients awaiting dialysis.
