Continuous monitoring of environmental impact and vector activity informed adjustments in collection schedules and station locations. These measures collectively mitigated health risks and supported overall recovery efforts, maintaining a clean and safe environment for affected populations.

Rapid waste management interventions were implemented to prevent accumulation in areas affected by recent slope failures. Temporary storage containers were deployed near shelters and food distribution points to minimize contamination risks. Teams conducted routine removal of organic and inorganic waste, with priority given to high-density zones. Residents were provided with guidance on proper disposal practices and the segregation of hazardous materials. Inspections ensured that containers remained functional and accessible, and adjustments were made based on crowding and shifting population clusters. These actions reduced environmental health hazards and supported the broader emergency response objectives.

Field teams focused on maintaining hygienic conditions by implementing organized waste collection and removal systems in newly established displacement sites. Temporary bins were placed to facilitate separation of biodegradable and non-biodegradable waste. Daily monitoring of container capacity and safe disposal ensured minimal accumulation near living and food preparation areas. Volunteers received training on handling waste safely, preventing contamination of water sources, and minimizing exposure to disease vectors. Site layouts were adjusted periodically to accommodate shifting population densities and ensure continued accessibility to collection points, maintaining a safe and sanitary environment.

Following landslide events, waste management was prioritized to minimize health hazards. Field teams conducted rapid assessments to identify areas where debris and refuse accumulated near shelters and access routes. Portable bins and collection points were deployed, with segregation of hazardous and non-hazardous waste enforced. Daily monitoring ensured removal of overflow materials, while educational initiatives informed residents of safe disposal practices. Coordination with transport and treatment services facilitated timely disposal of collected waste, preventing contamination of water sources and reducing the risk of communicable diseases in crowded living conditions.

In response to post-landslide displacement, a structured approach to waste management was implemented. Collection points were strategically located to ensure accessibility and prevent accumulation near shelters and water sources. Staff oversaw daily waste segregation, separating organic material for composting and non-organic items for safe disposal. Community members were engaged in monitoring and reporting, promoting adherence to sanitation guidelines. Routine inspections allowed rapid identification of overfilled containers or areas requiring additional coverage. These interventions supported hygienic living conditions, reduced vector-borne disease risk, and facilitated environmental protection during the initial recovery phase.

Efficient management of solid waste was established in emergency-affected zones to maintain sanitary conditions. Collection points were positioned to optimize access while reducing the likelihood of contamination. Daily monitoring and removal schedules were enforced, with hazardous materials segregated and safely transported to treatment facilities. Educational signage and community briefings reinforced proper disposal techniques and the importance of hygiene. Coordination with local authorities ensured timely transportation of collected waste to designated sites. This systematic approach minimized environmental and public health risks, supporting sustained sanitation practices during the emergency response period.

Following recent slope failures, health surveillance teams noted clusters of acute watery diarrhea in temporary shelters. Investigations indicated potential contamination of water sources due to disrupted sanitation infrastructure. Field officers emphasized rapid identification of symptomatic cases and mapping of high-risk zones, particularly where displaced populations rely on untreated surface water. Early warning indicators include rising patient visits to health posts with gastrointestinal symptoms, coupled with environmental assessments showing compromised water storage and sanitation facilities. Prompt reporting of these indicators is critical to initiate preventative measures such as safe water distribution, hygiene promotion, and targeted oral rehydration therapy deployment to reduce morbidity and prevent escalation of disease transmission.

In the aftermath of landslides, surveillance systems flagged elevated reports of diarrhea in affected communities. Field teams monitored indicators such as sudden increases in clinic visits, water turbidity, and proximity to flood-contaminated latrines. Rapid assessment of these factors helped identify potential cholera emergence zones. Containment measures included ensuring safe drinking water availability, reinforcing hygiene messaging, and prepositioning treatment supplies. Health workers were advised to document patient locations and symptom onset times to track possible outbreak patterns. Timely identification of these triggers allowed authorities to implement focused interventions before widespread transmission occurred, minimizing both human health impact and strain on emergency medical resources.

Field teams responding to landslide-affected areas observed early warning signs of cholera through routine water quality testing and patient symptom monitoring. Indicators such as elevated fecal coliform counts, reports of acute diarrhea, and compromised sanitation facilities prompted the initiation of emergency health protocols. Rapid deployment of water purification units, safe water storage containers, and hygiene kits was prioritized in high-density shelters. Health workers conducted door-to-door surveillance to identify potential cases early, while community volunteers were trained to recognize dehydration symptoms and report suspect cases. These combined measures allowed for timely preventive action, reducing the likelihood of sustained outbreaks and supporting overall public health resilience in post-disaster conditions.

After slope failures caused displacement, environmental health assessments identified high-risk areas where open defecation and contaminated water sources overlapped. Surveillance teams tracked unusual patterns of gastrointestinal complaints in clinics and shelters, which served as early triggers for potential cholera transmission. Rapid mobilization included chlorination of community water points, promotion of handwashing with soap, and distribution of oral rehydration salts. Field monitoring emphasized timely reporting of patient clustering and water contamination events. Lessons from prior incidents underscore the importance of integrating epidemiological surveillance with sanitation and hygiene interventions to prevent outbreaks during the critical initial phase of post-landslide emergency response.

In post-landslide settings, monitoring waterborne disease indicators is essential for early detection of cholera risks. Key triggers include elevated cases of acute watery diarrhea among children, reports of contaminated water sources, and insufficient sanitation facilities in shelters. Teams were instructed to map water collection points, observe hygiene practices, and perform rapid water testing. Early intervention involved prepositioning rehydration materials, establishing isolation protocols for severe cases, and coordinating with local authorities to improve access to safe water. Continuous reporting of these indicators enables emergency managers to implement control measures promptly, preventing outbreak amplification and safeguarding vulnerable populations.

Following landslide-induced displacement, early warning signs of cholera were monitored through combined epidemiological and environmental indicators. Indicators included sudden spikes in gastrointestinal complaints at health posts, reports of shallow water contamination, and overcrowded living conditions with inadequate latrine access. Rapid response teams conducted focused investigations, ensuring safe water points were functional and hygiene education campaigns were intensified. Community health volunteers were trained to identify dehydration symptoms and report suspected cases promptly. The integration of field surveillance with preventive measures facilitated rapid containment, reducing the potential for widespread transmission in high-risk settlements.

During the initial recovery phase after slope failures, cholera outbreak triggers were identified using multiple surveillance streams. Indicators such as rising diarrhea cases among children under five, malfunctioning sanitation facilities, and standing water near shelters prompted rapid preventive measures. Teams prioritized water purification, distribution of hygiene kits, and establishment of community-based reporting mechanisms. Health workers monitored trends daily, while local volunteers supported identification of at-risk individuals. Prompt recognition and response to these signals allowed health authorities to prevent escalation and protect vulnerable populations, ensuring that post-disaster recovery proceeded without secondary health crises.

Health teams in landslide-affected settlements employed a tiered monitoring approach to detect potential cholera emergence. Key indicators included clustering of acute watery diarrhea, reports of unprotected water sources, and observed lapses in sanitation practices. Rapid deployment of water treatment units, hygiene education campaigns, and oral rehydration therapy kits were prioritized in response to these triggers. Community engagement facilitated early identification of symptomatic cases, while ongoing environmental assessments allowed teams to target interventions effectively. This proactive strategy minimized disease propagation and ensured a structured approach to outbreak prevention in high-density temporary shelters.

Post-landslide displacement sites were assessed for early signs of waterborne disease risk. Surveillance highlighted increased gastrointestinal complaints coinciding with contaminated water points and overused latrines. Field teams initiated preemptive interventions including provision of chlorinated water, oral rehydration salts, and hygiene promotion campaigns. Community health volunteers were trained to recognize dehydration and track incidence rates. Rapid documentation of these indicators allowed for timely alerting of medical teams and activation of containment measures, preventing escalation into larger outbreaks and supporting safe living conditions during the recovery phase.

Following landslide events, early warning of cholera relied on close monitoring of both human and environmental indicators. Surveillance teams tracked sudden increases in acute diarrhea, contamination of communal water sources, and overcrowding near temporary shelters. Preemptive measures included rapid deployment of safe water points, distribution of hygiene supplies, and establishment of monitoring protocols for new cases. Coordination with local health facilities ensured that suspect cases were recorded and treated promptly. These procedures enabled rapid response, limiting transmission potential and maintaining public health integrity during critical post-disaster periods.

Following recent slope failures, stagnant water accumulated in depressions and temporary shelters, creating favorable conditions for vector proliferation. Field teams conducted systematic mapping of breeding sites and prioritized drainage or larvicidal interventions. Community awareness campaigns emphasized eliminating standing water containers and maintaining clean surroundings to reduce vector density. Environmental health officers coordinated with local sanitation crews to ensure timely removal of debris and debris-induced water pooling. These measures aimed to limit vector-borne disease risk, particularly for mosquito-transmitted infections, while maintaining routine surveillance to detect any resurgence promptly. Early deployment of protective measures and continuous monitoring were critical to safeguarding vulnerable populations from vector-related health hazards during post-landslide recovery.

In landslide-affected areas, temporary shelters and disrupted drainage systems created new vector habitats. Rapid assessments identified open containers, puddles, and partially covered water storage as breeding grounds. Vector control teams deployed larvicide treatments and advised communities on personal protective measures such as insecticide-treated nets. Coordination with health units ensured timely reporting of vector-borne illness cases and enabled focused interventions in high-density settlements. Routine environmental surveillance was established to track effectiveness of control measures, with priority given to high-risk zones exhibiting stagnated water accumulation. Preventive strategies also included proper waste disposal and clearing blocked drainage channels to reduce potential vector breeding.

Post-landslide conditions led to extensive water pooling in landslide scar zones and along disrupted roads, creating potential breeding sites for vectors. Environmental health teams mapped these high-risk areas and conducted larval inspections. Household education focused on emptying containers, covering water storage, and maintaining sanitation perimeters around shelters. Targeted fogging and larvicidal interventions were applied in identified hotspots. Data from vector surveillance informed ongoing operational priorities and allowed rapid reallocation of resources to newly emerging breeding sites. Continuous monitoring ensured that preventive measures were adapted to changing environmental conditions, reducing the risk of vector-borne disease transmission in affected communities.

Following slope failures, vector populations were closely monitored in temporary settlements. Accumulated rainwater and debris created new larval habitats, particularly for mosquitoes capable of transmitting arboviral infections. Field teams implemented routine inspections of standing water, distributed larvicide treatments, and facilitated community-led clean-up activities. Health volunteers received training to recognize vector breeding risks and report hotspots to the central response coordination unit. Preventive measures were complemented by distribution of bed nets and the promotion of personal protection practices. This proactive approach minimized disease transmission risk, enabling communities to stabilize shelter conditions while maintaining environmental hygiene standards in post-landslide recovery zones.

During the recovery phase after landslides, stagnant water and debris accumulation required focused vector management. Environmental health officers identified high-density settlements where uncollected waste and open water containers were prevalent. Control measures included systematic removal of debris, larvicide applications, and strategic drainage improvements. Community members were engaged to maintain clean surroundings, regularly inspect household water containers, and implement protective measures. Monitoring teams tracked vector densities and seasonal breeding patterns to inform adaptive interventions. These coordinated strategies minimized the risk of vector-borne disease outbreaks and contributed to safer post-disaster environments.

Post-landslide field assessments highlighted areas with persistent water accumulation, ideal for vector breeding. Surveillance teams prioritized household inspections and mapped high-risk environmental pockets. Interventions included drainage improvements, distribution of larvicidal agents, and awareness campaigns emphasizing elimination of standing water. Health officers coordinated with shelter managers to reduce water pooling around communal facilities. Data collection on vector presence and environmental conditions informed ongoing deployment of control measures. Routine follow-ups ensured sustainability of preventive actions, mitigating the risk of mosquito-borne and other vector-associated illnesses during post-disaster recovery.

In landslide-affected regions, vector surveillance identified stagnant water and debris-laden zones as potential breeding grounds. Emergency teams implemented larvicide treatments, ensured regular emptying of containers, and reinforced community-level hygiene education. Particular attention was given to shelters with limited drainage and areas adjacent to water collection points. Coordinated monitoring of mosquito larvae and other vectors allowed teams to adjust interventions dynamically. The combination of environmental management, chemical control, and community participation effectively reduced vector proliferation, lowering the risk of associated diseases and safeguarding vulnerable populations in temporary settlements.

Following heavy slope failures, water collection in natural depressions and temporary shelters created vector breeding hotspots. Rapid field surveys were conducted to identify stagnant pools, clogged drainage systems, and open water containers. Teams deployed larvicide treatments, distributed insecticide-treated nets, and educated households on preventive practices. Continuous monitoring enabled adaptive responses to newly formed breeding sites, ensuring high-risk areas were treated promptly. Collaboration with local volunteers ensured sustainable control measures and rapid reporting of vector activity, preventing potential outbreaks of vector-borne illnesses during the initial recovery period.

Environmental assessments in post-landslide areas revealed extensive water retention around shelters and debris, contributing to vector breeding potential. Response teams prioritized clearing debris, enhancing drainage, and applying larvicides to standing water. Household-level education campaigns promoted elimination of open water containers and maintenance of hygienic perimeters. Surveillance data was collected regularly to identify trends in vector presence and adjust interventions accordingly. These proactive measures reduced the risk of vector-borne disease transmission, ensuring a safer living environment for displaced populations while maintaining effective post-disaster recovery operations.

Following landslide events, water accumulation and environmental disruption created opportunities for vector proliferation. Control measures included mapping stagnant water, applying larvicides to breeding sites, and educating communities on protective actions such as container management and personal protection. Shelter inspections focused on eliminating debris and ensuring proper drainage. Health teams coordinated data collection on vector density and monitored any emergence of vector-borne illnesses. Integration of environmental management with preventive interventions helped minimize the risk of outbreaks, supporting the safety and health of affected populations during the post-disaster response and recovery phases.

The appointed Cluster Lead for any sector is responsible for chairing regular coordination meetings with all operational partners. The primary goal is to establish a '4W' matrix (Who is doing What, Where, and When) to map response activities, identify gaps, and prevent duplication of aid efforts in the landslide-affected area.

The Health Cluster Lead must coordinate with health partners to establish a clear patient referral pathway from landslide sites. This includes mapping the operational status of nearby clinics and hospitals and organizing medical evacuation (MEDEVAC) for critically injured survivors who cannot be treated at field clinics.

The Shelter Cluster Lead, often the IFRC or a partner agency, is tasked with coordinating the distribution of emergency shelter kits and non-food items (NFIs). They must ensure that distribution plans are harmonized among all partners to maintain equity and avoid creating tensions within the displaced community.

The WASH (Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene) Cluster Lead must coordinate rapid technical assessments of water sources in and around the landslide area. Their priority is to identify uncontaminated water points and organize the immediate provision of water purification supplies to prevent outbreaks of waterborne diseases like cholera.

The National Disaster Management Centre (NDMC) serves as the government's focal point for coordinating with international and national Cluster Leads. All cluster strategies and response plans must be presented to and aligned with the NDMC's overall national response framework for the landslide disaster.

The Protection Cluster Lead is responsible for advocating with all other clusters to ensure the safety and dignity of affected populations. This includes advising the Shelter Cluster on safe site planning to mitigate risks of gender-based violence (GBV) in displacement camps.

The Logistics Cluster Lead must coordinate with local authorities and security forces to establish secure and accessible routes for humanitarian convoys. A key task is to communicate planned movements to all partners to maximize cargo space and ensure timely delivery of aid to isolated landslide victims.

For effective coordination, each humanitarian Cluster Lead should have a designated counterpart or co-lead from the relevant Local Government department. This partnership ensures that response activities are culturally appropriate, leverage local knowledge, and support existing government systems.

All Cluster Leads are required to provide standardized inputs for the inter-agency Situation Reports (SitReps) compiled by UN OCHA. These inputs must include key achievements, identified needs, and operational constraints within their sector for the reporting period.

The Early Recovery Cluster Lead is tasked with coordinating multi-sectoral assessments to plan for post-disaster needs. This involves liaising with other cluster leads to transition from immediate relief to sustainable recovery projects, such as livelihood support and infrastructure rehabilitation, once the initial landslide crisis subsides.

Before collecting personally identifiable information (PII) from landslide survivors, responders must obtain informed consent. This requires clearly explaining what data is being collected, why it's needed (e.g., for aid registration), how it will be stored, and with whom it might be shared. Consent should be documented.

Adhere to the principle of data minimization. When registering landslide-affected populations, only collect the absolute minimum personal data necessary for the specific humanitarian purpose. Avoid collecting sensitive data (e.g., political affiliation, religion) unless it is essential for delivering specialized, targeted aid.

All digital records containing beneficiary data must be encrypted, both at rest (on device storage) and in transit (over a network). Use password-protected files and secure communication channels to transfer any sensitive information related to landslide victims.

Physical records containing personal data, such as paper registration forms or assessment notes, must be kept in a secure, locked container at all times. Access must be strictly limited to authorized personnel to prevent loss, theft, or unauthorized viewing of survivor information.

Implement a '3-2-1' backup strategy for all critical landslide response data. Maintain at least three copies of the data on two different storage media types, with at least one copy stored in a secure off-site location (e.g., a secure cloud server or a different physical office) to ensure recovery in case of equipment loss or damage.

For public health reporting and statistical analysis of landslide-related injuries and diseases, all personal identifiers must be removed from the dataset. Only share aggregated and anonymized health data to protect patient confidentiality while still enabling effective public health planning.

The data of unaccompanied and separated children affected by the landslide is extremely sensitive. Their identity and location data must be protected with the highest level of security and should never be shared publicly or across open channels to prevent trafficking, exploitation, and other protection risks.

Sharing personal data between different response agencies is prohibited unless a formal data sharing agreement is in place. This agreement must outline the legal basis for sharing, the specific data to be shared, security measures, and the purpose limitation for its use.

Personal data collected from landslide-affected populations must not be retained indefinitely. A clear data retention schedule must be established, and once the data is no longer needed for its original purpose, it must be securely disposed of through methods like shredding paper documents or cryptographic erasure of digital files.

Field teams must ensure the privacy of individuals when collecting data. This means conducting interviews or registration in a place where conversations cannot be easily overheard and shielding screens or paper forms from public view to protect the confidentiality of the person providing information.

The GBV referral pathway must be survivor-centered, prioritizing the survivor's rights, needs, and wishes. All referrals for services, whether medical, psychosocial, or legal, must be made only with the informed consent of the survivor. Their safety and confidentiality are the primary considerations.

Any non-specialized responder who receives a disclosure of GBV should listen with empathy, offer support, and provide information on available services. Do not ask for details or investigate the incident. Assure the survivor of confidentiality and refer them to a trained GBV focal point or service provider.

For sexual violence survivors, the referral pathway must include timely access to a health facility providing Clinical Management of Rape (CMR). This is a life-saving medical service that should ideally be accessed within 72 hours of the incident to prevent HIV, STIs, and unwanted pregnancy.

Strict confidentiality is non-negotiable in all GBV cases. Sharing any information about a survivor's case without their explicit consent can expose them to stigma, retribution, and further harm. All records must be stored securely and access must be restricted.

If the survivor is a child, the referral pathway must link to specialized child protection services. All actions must be guided by the 'best interests of the child' principle, and referrals should be managed by a designated child protection officer.

The first step for the Protection Cluster is to urgently map all available GBV services in the landslide-affected area. This map must be updated regularly and include contact information for health, psychosocial support, legal aid, and safe spaces, noting their current operational capacity.

A comprehensive referral pathway must include access to mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS). Following a traumatic event like a landslide, compounded by violence, survivors require specialized care to support their recovery in a safe and supportive environment.

Information about how to access the GBV referral pathway must be communicated safely and discreetly. Use channels that are accessible to women and girls, such as posters in female-only latrines, women's community groups, or through female community health workers.

If a GBV survivor's safety is at immediate risk from the perpetrator or community members, the referral pathway must have a clear protocol for access to an emergency shelter or safe house. This requires careful safety planning with the survivor.

When making a referral, only the minimum necessary information should be shared with the receiving service provider, and only with the survivor's consent. Use secure referral forms and avoid discussing case details over open communication channels like radio to protect confidentiality.

The immediate priority in any landslide-affected area is to identify and register all Unaccompanied and Separated Children (UASC). They must be brought to a safe location, provided with immediate care, and their details documented to initiate family tracing procedures without delay.

For every separated child, Family Tracing and Reunification (FTR) is the primary objective. This process must be systematic, confidential, and always verify family links before a reunification takes place to ensure the child is returned to a safe family environment.

All staff, volunteers, and partners interacting with children must be trained on and sign a Code of Conduct that includes strict child safeguarding rules. Background checks should be conducted where possible. Any suspicion or disclosure of child abuse must be reported immediately through established confidential channels.

Establish Child-Friendly Spaces (CFS) in displacement sites and affected communities. These are safe, supervised environments where children can play, learn, and receive psychosocial support from trained facilitators, helping them cope with the trauma of the landslide.

Interim care for unaccompanied children should be community-based. Avoid placing children in institutional care (orphanages). The best alternative care options are placement with vetted relatives, trained foster families within the community, or in small, supervised group settings.

The principle of the 'best interests of the child' must guide every decision and action. This means prioritizing a child's safety, physical and emotional well-being, and their right to development in all response activities, from food distribution to shelter planning.

Be vigilant for increased risks of child trafficking, child labor, and early marriage following a landslide due to family separation and loss of livelihoods. Response programs must ensure aid does not inadvertently increase these risks and should include community awareness messaging on child protection.

A structured case management system is required for children at high risk. This involves assigning a dedicated caseworker to assess the child's needs, develop a care plan, make referrals to specialized services (health, MHPSS), and conduct follow-ups to ensure their safety and well-being.

Ensure children's safe access to all humanitarian services. This includes locating water points, latrines, and distribution areas in well-lit, centrally located, and easily accessible places to minimize the risk of violence, especially for adolescent girls.

Promote child participation in the response. Create safe and ethical opportunities for children and adolescents to share their views on their needs, the risks they face, and the design of programs intended for them, ensuring their voices are heard and considered in the landslide response.

Search and rescue operations must be prepared to assist and evacuate persons with disabilities. Teams should be trained to identify signs of disability and have protocols for safely moving individuals with mobility limitations from landslide-damaged structures and unstable terrain.

All public information related to the landslide, including evacuation notices, safety warnings, and aid distribution schedules, must be communicated in accessible formats. This includes using simple language, large text, audio messages, and sign language interpreters to reach people with different types of impairments.

Emergency shelters must be designed to be inclusive. This requires installing ramps for wheelchair access, ensuring toilets and washing facilities are accessible, and allocating safe, easy-to-reach spaces within the shelter for persons with disabilities and their families.

During needs assessments and registration, proactively identify persons with disabilities. Use the Washington Group Short Set of Questions (WG-SS) to collect data on functional limitations in a dignified way, ensuring their specific needs are factored into the response.

Prioritize the replacement of assistive devices like wheelchairs, crutches, hearing aids, and eyeglasses that were lost or damaged in the landslide. The loss of these devices is a critical protection risk and severely limits a person's mobility, independence, and access to services.

Make aid distribution points physically accessible and safe. Establish a separate, clearly marked, and faster queue for persons with disabilities, older persons, and pregnant women. If access is a barrier, arrange for direct delivery of relief items to their shelters.

Child protection services must be inclusive of children with disabilities, who are at extreme risk of harm, neglect, and abandonment after a disaster. Child-Friendly Spaces should have trained staff and accessible materials to ensure their full participation and safety.

Ensure persons with disabilities and their representative organizations (DPOs) are actively involved in all stages of the disaster response. Their participation in planning and feedback sessions is crucial for identifying barriers and creating effective, inclusive solutions.

All humanitarian staff and volunteers must be trained on the principles of disability inclusion. This includes using rights-based language, understanding different types of disabilities, and knowing how to provide reasonable accommodation in their service delivery.

Mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS) must be accessible to everyone. Ensure counselors are trained to support individuals with intellectual or communication disabilities, and that services are provided in locations that are physically accessible to people with mobility impairments.

Check for and document any essential medications the elderly person needs for chronic conditions (e.g., diabetes, hypertension, heart disease). Verify if they have at least a 72-hour supply. If not, report this as a critical medical need immediately.

Check the individual's immediate safety and physical condition. Assess for injuries from the landslide and for environmental risks like unstable ground. If the person has limited mobility, ensure they are in a safe, accessible location away from further harm.

Check for mobility aids. Ascertain if the person uses a cane, walker, or wheelchair and if these items were lost or damaged in the landslide. Lack of these devices is a major barrier to accessing services and must be reported for replacement.

Check for hydration and nutrition status. Offer water immediately. Ask about their last meal and any specific dietary requirements or difficulties with chewing or swallowing. Elderly individuals are at high risk of rapid dehydration.

Check if the person has been separated from their family or caregiver. If they are alone, initiate the Restoring Family Links (RFL) protocol by carefully documenting their details and the names of relatives to locate their support network.

Check for sensory aids like eyeglasses and hearing aids. If these are missing, the person's ability to receive important safety information is compromised. When communicating, speak clearly, slowly, and face the person directly.

Check for signs of confusion, disorientation, or severe emotional distress. The trauma of a landslide can exacerbate cognitive conditions. Provide psychological first aid by listening patiently and ensuring they are not left alone if they seem distressed.

Check that their designated shelter space is appropriate. It should be on level ground, located near to toilets, and in a quieter area of the camp if possible to ensure they can rest and move about safely.

Check that the person has received and understood information about aid distributions and available services. Do not assume they have heard general announcements. Verbally provide clear, simple information and confirm their understanding.

Check that the person is registered for follow-up visits by community health volunteers or social workers. Regular check-ins are crucial to monitor their well-being and identify any new needs that arise during the recovery period.

All spontaneous (unaffiliated) volunteers must be directed to a designated Volunteer Reception Centre (VRC). The first step of verification is mandatory registration, which includes presenting a valid government-issued photo ID to confirm their identity.

Verification of claimed technical skills is mandatory. Before assigning a volunteer to a specialized role (e.g., medical aid, structural assessment), staff must see and confirm their professional license, certification, or other official credentials.

Any volunteer who will have direct contact with children or other vulnerable groups must undergo a rapid background check. This is a non-negotiable child safeguarding protocol to prevent potential harm.

Once verified, every volunteer must be issued an official, weather-proof identification card and/or a branded vest. This ID must be worn and be clearly visible at all times to identify them as an authorized responder to the community and other agencies.

Issue public safety announcements advising landslide survivors to only accept direct assistance from volunteers who can present their official agency-issued identification. This helps protect the affected population from fraud or exploitation.

A crucial part of the verification process is ensuring each volunteer attends a mandatory orientation. This session must cover the Code of Conduct, safety protocols specific to the landslide site, and the principle of 'do no harm'.

For volunteers arriving with a pre-registered NGO or partner agency, the team leader must present a letter of assignment and a roster of their members. The legitimacy of the organization itself should be verified against the coordination body's list of approved partners.

Verify a volunteer's physical fitness and capabilities before assigning them to strenuous tasks. A simple self-declaration of health and a visual assessment can prevent injuries and ensure tasks are matched to the individual's ability in the challenging landslide environment.

Implement a formal checkout process for all volunteers at the end of their deployment. This final verification step includes a debriefing, collecting feedback, and crucially, retrieving all official identification materials to prevent their misuse.

The primary decision rule for using cash assistance is market functionality. A rapid assessment must confirm that local markets are open, accessible, and stocked with essential goods at stable prices. If markets are destroyed by the landslide, in-kind aid is necessary.

The security situation is a key decision rule. If distributing physical cash would expose landslide survivors or aid workers to significant risks of theft or violence, a safer modality like vouchers, mobile money, or in-kind assistance must be chosen.

The preference of the affected population is a crucial decision factor. To ensure dignified aid, consult with survivors about whether they prefer cash to meet their own diverse needs or in-kind assistance for specific items. This feedback should heavily influence program design.

Rule for immediate response: In the first 24-72 hours after a major landslide when information is scarce and access is blocked, the default response is often life-saving in-kind aid (e.g., water, food, blankets) as market conditions are unknown or unstable.

A key decision rule relates to the type of need. For specific technical or health commodities not available in local markets (e.g., therapeutic foods, water purification tablets), targeted in-kind distribution is superior to cash.

The decision to use cash depends on the availability of financial infrastructure. If banks, mobile money agents, or other cash-out points are damaged or non-existent in the landslide-affected area, the logistical challenges may require a shift to in-kind aid.

Consider using commodity vouchers as a decision rule when the goal is to restrict purchases to a specific category of items (e.g., shelter materials) while still supporting local vendors and providing beneficiary choice within that category.

A binding decision rule is government acceptance. All cash or voucher assistance programs must be approved by and coordinated with the relevant national or local authorities. If government policy prohibits or does not support CVA, in-kind assistance must be used.

The rule of speed and access: When roads are blocked by debris but local markets in unaffected nearby areas are functional, cash delivered via mobile transfer can often reach survivors faster than physically transporting in-kind goods.

A final decision rule involves a cost-efficiency analysis. Compare the total cost of delivering cash (including transfer fees) against the full logistical cost of in-kind aid (procurement, transport, warehousing, distribution) to determine which modality is a better use of funds.

The initial beneficiary verification process should involve community leaders. Create preliminary lists of landslide-affected households based on their knowledge, which are then formally verified by registration teams to ensure accuracy and inclusion of all affected families.

During registration, verify a household's identity using a government-issued ID. If an ID was lost in the landslide, accept credible alternatives such as a statement from a village chief, a utility bill with a matching address, or other forms of documentation.

To prevent aid duplication, each verified household must be assigned a unique ID number. This number should be recorded in a central database and used by all responding agencies to track what assistance the household has received.

At the distribution point, final verification is mandatory. The beneficiary must present their registration token or card, and their identity must be checked against the master list before aid is disbursed. A signature or fingerprint should be collected as proof of receipt.

For vulnerable persons without any ID or family to vouch for them, establish a verification committee. This committee, comprising agency staff and trusted community leaders, can assess individual cases to ensure they are not excluded from receiving essential aid.

Before finalizing a beneficiary list for distribution, it must be cross-referenced with lists from other organizations working in the same landslide-affected area. This de-duplication process is a crucial step to ensure equitable aid distribution.

To promote transparency and community-led verification, post a non-sensitive version of the beneficiary list in a public location. Provide a clear and safe process for community members to report any errors or omissions they observe.

A functional and accessible complaints and feedback mechanism must be in place. This allows individuals who feel they were incorrectly excluded during verification to raise their case for review, ensuring accountability.

Conduct Post-Distribution Monitoring (PDM) by visiting a random sample of households after they receive aid. This final verification step confirms that the assistance reached the intended beneficiaries and helps identify any potential inclusion or exclusion errors.

When possible, use biometric data (e.g., fingerprints) collected during the initial registration for verification at distribution points. This is one of the most effective methods to reduce fraud and ensure aid is given only to registered beneficiaries.

Upon receipt of any goods at the warehouse, a Goods Received Note (GRN) must be raised. This document must detail the item, quantity, donor, and condition on arrival. This is the first and most critical step for all incoming inventory.

A separate stock card must be maintained for every single line item (SKU) in the warehouse. Every movement of that item, whether received or dispatched, must be recorded on its specific stock card to maintain a running balance.

No stock should leave the warehouse without an authorized waybill or dispatch request form. This document acts as proof of handover and must specify the items, quantities, destination, and receiving person or agency.

For all items with an expiration date, such as medical supplies and nutritional products, the First-In, First-Out (FIFO) inventory principle must be strictly enforced to prevent wastage of critical supplies.

At the close of each day, a physical spot check of several high-turnover items must be conducted. The physical count must be reconciled with the balances on the stock cards to identify and correct any discrepancies immediately.

Any inventory that is damaged, for instance by water ingress at a warehouse near the landslide zone, or found to be expired must be formally recorded on a loss report. This stock must be segregated and removed from the available inventory count.

A consolidated stock report showing the inventory levels of all key relief items must be submitted to the central coordinating body (e.g., Logistics Cluster) on a regular, agreed-upon schedule. This is essential for pipeline visibility and planning.

Access to the warehouse and storage areas must be strictly controlled and limited to authorized personnel only. This is a fundamental security measure to protect relief supplies from theft and ensure inventory records remain accurate.

When assembling kits, such as family hygiene kits, the individual components must be deducted from their respective stock cards. The completed kits are then added to the inventory as a new item, ensuring accurate tracking of all materials.

For temporary forward staging areas located closer to the landslide-affected population, a simplified paper-based ledger system can be used to track inventory. However, all transfers to and from the main warehouse must still be documented with waybills.
